Linda Brakel's new book adopts a multidimensional approach to transgender experience: conceptual, analogical, and quasi-empirical. Brakel decides not to focus on the subjectivity of trans experience, as such. As she puts it, absent will be “the always interesting, often compelling, personal and clinical accounts of and by Trans persons” (p. 5). While appreciating the value of such first-person contributions, Brakel hopes her own contribution will come from other directions.

Self. One of Brakel's goals is to consider answers to three questions about the self, often referred to within philosophy as part of personal identity theory. Brakel's questions are “ontological,” “epistemological,” and “conceptual” (p. 12). She anticipates in her introduction (chapter 1) that neither classical (chapter 2), nor modern (chapter 3), nor contemporary gender theorists’ concepts of self (chapter 4), alone or combined, will be able to answer the three questions she poses (p.15) or provide solutions for the apparent Moore's paradox problems of trans experience. She calls many accounts flatly “disappointing” (p. 66). And in a frank summary of the book's achievements (chapter 8), Brakel concludes, “on the whole, I must confess that in this short monograph I have raised many more questions than I have been able to answer” (p. 171). Given the limited positive results, I will pass over the details of what Brakel covers in those chapters to focus elsewhere.

Rachel Widawsky  

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Series of Reviews (Continued)

In this issue of JAPA Review of Books, we are delighted to continue our review series of The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott with an excellent review of Volume 10 by Jack Foehl and Christopher Bonovitz. Covering the last two years of Winnicott's life, this teaching volume offers twenty-one clinical cases where the reader will discover what Foehl and Bonovitz describe as “the magic of Winnicott exploring the unknown territory of a new case.” Shepherded by guest editors Adrienne Harris and Phillip Blumberg, Foehl and Bonovitz bring Winnicott's wit, wisdom, and clinical acumen to our pages.

Psychoanalysis and Climate Change

“Tell me,” the poet Mary Oliver (1992) famously wrote, “what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?” In these past summer months, so many of us, if we were able, retreated to our own wild places to restore and refresh ourselves. Craving sustenance, renewal, hope for the future, and a sense of well-being and connection, we likely each noticed, in our travels, the environmental ravages of human activity: erosion of our beaches, changes in the wildlife we glimpsed, blighted foliage. Our brains are surely wired to respond with awe to the beauty of unspoiled nature, and we are grief-stricken to see the destruction of our vanishing landscapes.

Why, then, are we such poor stewards, such careless and uncaring inhabitants, seemingly so helpless in the face of undeniable signs of our planet's impending ruin? It is in part, as we psychoanalysts know, because humans are masterful deniers of reality when reality disturbs our equilibrium.

“Familiar Artifice”: Ways of Telling in the Short Story, Psychoanalysis, and Alice Munro’s “The Moons of Jüpiter”

Rosemary Rizq

Rosemary Rizq

London SW15 4JD
UNITED KINGDOM
Email: r.rizq@roehampton.ac.uk
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8875-1735

Rosemary Rizq  

Alice Munro, fable, identification, narrative coherence, short story, Totem and Taboo

Theoretical ideas about “narrative coherence” and “autobiographical competence” remain prevalent in contemporary therapeutic culture, and are frequently deployed in the service of the patient's producing a narrative “I” that can tell its own story. A preference for novelistic accounts of the self is countered here by proposing the short story form as an alternative model for the telling of a self within psychoanalysis. Alice Munro's “The Moons of Jupiter,” the roots of the short story form in fable, and a rereading of Freud's Totem and Taboo are used to illuminate how the short story may be seen as an exemplary tale paralleling the origin of the self in its identification with the other.

This gem of a book overflows with wisdom accumulated over the decades of Nancy McWilliams's experience doing psychodynamic supervision, as well as with crucial insights about psychodynamic treatment itself. The author has a rare combination of brilliance and modesty (including about her own errors), which smooths our way into putting her advice into practice. In contrast, I once had a supervisor who seemed so smug that I was careful to use his sometimes helpful advice only with other patients, not wanting to further inflate his ego. A fellow candidate told me she had a similar supervisor, so she never told him when his overconfident clinical predictions proved wrong.

The book is well organized in nine chapters that cover various aspects of supervision, including group supervision. When she does group supervision, McWilliams often credits young supervisees with knowing more than their older supervisors about relevant group dynamics, as well as about cultural sensitivity. She observes that “many people of color in the United States … are currently finding that the emotional burden of educating white clinicians about racism has become exhausting” (p. 181). Her final chapter offers advice to trainees about getting the most out of supervision.

The preface whets the reader's appetite for what is to come. Like the rest of the book, it is engagingly personal. McWilliams credits mentors who encouraged her highly successful writing career. She speaks of her mother's death when she was nine, and its pivotal influence in leading her to value not only the new, but also the old, rather than fall victim to exclusive neophilia. She is also deeply interested in our professional literature on supervision (and treatment), digesting and citing it at every turn. Avoiding jargon, she focuses on supervision as a means of “promoting maturation, confidence, and a sense of agency in the therapist… . the emphasis on wisdom and clinical experience is more reflective of clinical realities” than is the increasing use of measurable learning objectives (p. xi). She wisely notes that “a penchant toward secrecy is the enemy of clinical maturation” (p. 167).

This “mini-book” (p. xix) by the renowned British analyst Patrick Casement is unlikely to become as widely known as its comparably slim sisters—Voltaire's Candide, Conrad's Heart of Darkness, Kama's “Metamorphosis.” and Saint-Exupery's The Little Prince—all of which run fewer than a hundred pages. However, this somber prediction must not detract from the fact that Casement's Credo? is a work of great luminosity, grace, and wisdom.

[[RunningHead]]

Eros, Thanatos, Agon

Robin Holloway

Robin Holloway

45 Sheppard Avenue East, Suite 202
Toronto, Ontario M2N 5W9
CANADA
Email: rholloway@thewillowcentre.com

Robin Holloway  

When I dig into the many and varied motivations that make me write, three Greek words come to mind: Eros, Thanatos, Agon. Freud's courageous introduction of Greek mythology into psychoanalytic theory has made us familiar with the first two. The Greek word agon (άγών) has the meanings of “contest, struggle, conflict, fight.” It is the etymological root of the English word agony.

My writing evolves from a gamut of emotions and motivations, with love and hate leading the way. For me, love of writing includes generosity, sharing, gratitude, and a deep desire for responsive engagement with others. Hate (Thanatos) joins hands with envy, greed, anxiety, and narcissistic exhibitionism.

Writing is a sexual act. It is driven from inside. It pressures from inside—a force and an urge demanding expression. What we write must also have sexual qualities to survive and to be alive. It must reek with desire for intercourse with receptive readers. What we write must be sexually fertile enough to generate offspring—offspring in the form of new feelings, new ideas, and new responses in our readers. What we write should also seek to engage in acts of “textual intercourse” with the texts of others, acts in which fertile texts tumble together and interpenetrate, entering into and joyfully receiving one another.

Hatred enters into my writing. I hate the task of attempting to communicate in words the swirl of complicated emotional responses and reveries that swamp my mind during a session. As T. S. Eliot (1944) reminds us, “Words strain, crack and sometimes break under the burden.” I especially hate the professionally imposed burden of cranking out a “process note” after every session. That this task is not internally driven but externally imposed creates anger and resentment. It offends my narcissistic wish to “get it right” and to capture in words the emotional core

[[RunningHead]]

What Happens When a Trans Patient Happens

Brian Kloppenberg

Brian Kloppenberg

1133 Broadway, Suite 1127
New York, NY10010
Email: bakloppenberg@aol.com

Brian Kloppenberg  

transphobia, psychoanalysis, neutrality

Transphobia, now emerging as a limit to the analytic attitude, requires the kind of psychoanalytic investigation that continues in response to misogyny, homophobia, and biphobia, as they figure in both clinical practice and theory building. Without a psychoanalytic investigation into the dynamics of transphobia in psychoanalysis, efforts to conceptualize trans subjects in an analytically open and neutral manner must falter. An exploration of the author's own efforts to overcome transphobic bias, as well as an analysis of transphobic dynamics in a well-known case in the literature, show some of the challenges that arise when attempts at thinking psychoanalytically get caught up in conscious and unconscious forms of transphobia.

[[RunningHead]]

On Revenge, Pardon, and Forgiveness

Dana Amir

Dana Amir

54 Sderot Hanassi Street
Haifa 346431
ISRAEL
Email: dana.amir2@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1574-5844

Dana Amir  

political violence, banal evil, doer / done to dynamics, revenge, forgiveness

Political violence has long been a focus of psychoanalytic writing. Jessica Benjamin's concepts of the complementarity, or twoness, and that of thirdness can serve as a point of departure allowing us to challenge the dichotomy between positions of revenge and forgiveness (on the victim's part) and positions of evil and remorse (on the perpetrator's), and to suggest a spectrum of encounters between victims and perpetrators that includes four types of interaction: (1) between the position of extreme evil on the perpetrator's part, and the position of revenge on the victim's; (2) between the perpetrator's position of banal evil, and the victim's position of false pardon; (3) between the perpetrator's position of guilt and atonement, and the victim's position of full pardon; (4) between the position of remorse on the perpetrator's part, and the position of forgiveness on the victim's. A thorough analysis of various testimonies illustrates how these four positions manifest, with varying degrees of dominance, in every encounter (whether concrete or imagined) between victim and perpetrator.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Mystic and the Interpretive Word
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Mystic and the Interpretive Word

Alice Nes

Alice Nes

Tirza 6/8 Ramat Gan 5236414
ISRAEL
Email: alicebar@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5549-7486

Alice Nes  

unconscious, mystical, communication, interpretation, verbal, nonverbal, intuition, psychoanalysis, William James

Explicit and implicit psychoanalytic assumptions concerning the analytic cure include the old “insight/interpretation” versus “relation/experience” duality. A synthesis of these two stances, grounded in recognition of the long denied yet central mystical facet of psychoanalysis and the crucial role of words in the “talking cure” that psychoanalysis still is, recognizes these two aspects of psychoanalysis—mystical communication through psychic overlap and interpretive words—as deeply interdependent. Analytic interpretations emerge from the depths of a mystical experience of psychic unity (as well as separateness) resulting from, but also creating, the patient-therapist caesura. Words shape the contour of this closeness-separateness matrix on which psychoanalysis depends. Moreover, the moment of insight into the psychic reality of the other is shown to often depend on crossing the threshold of the nonverbal toward consciousness and language. Constant movement between verbalization and the nonverbal is illustrated with clinical vignettes stressing the interplay of the mystical and the symbolized, of interpretation and intuition.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Writing to be Part of the Conversation

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue, Suite 2220
Chicago, IL 60601
Email: bonlit@gmail.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

I first started to write in the 1970s because I had to. Academics live by the rule “publish or perish,” at least until receiving tenure. Even after that, publishing remains the major means for advancement, though failing to publish carries less serious consequences. Writing is part of the job, and the rules for what kind of writing, on which topics, where to publish, and so forth are quite explicit—straightforward enough to be taught to graduate students, which is also part of the job. In these circumstances, the “why” of writing is pragmatic.

More interesting is the question of why I write when I don't have to, when seemingly nothing external depends on my doing so. For during the same time that I was writing for work I was also writing and publishing papers that would not be included in my tenure dossier, papers outside the rules; and these papers were my first writings about psychoanalysis.

I had arrived at that point in my work with a focus on meaning, but that was not where I had started. Like most shy children, I was more comfortable listening than speaking; being quiet and well-behaved, I was allowed to be present as adults talked about both current world events and their personal affairs. Being very young, I am sure I did not understand all the nuances of what the adults said, but I definitely sensed that their words were just the topmost layer of deeper, more interesting meanings. Curiosity about those “enigmatic signifiers” (per Laplanche) compelled me to spend hours in my father's library, surrounded by books, many of which impressed on me that words have power and magic.

One favorite was a book on the Bible in art, in which a passage of the Bible was surrounded by reproductions of its depiction by various artists. Although I was told that a picture has the power of a thousand words, I could see for myself the power of words to create multiple worlds.

Former Editor, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

The Klein Tradition: Lines of Development—Evolution of Theory and Practice over the Decades. Edited by Penelope Garvey and Kay Long. New York: Routledge, 2018, xxxiv + 437 pp., $84.95 paperback.

Elizabeth Fritsch

Elizabeth Fritsch

6723 Whittier Avenue, Suite 307
McLean, VA 22101
Email: drelizabethfritsch@gmail.com

Elizabeth Fritsch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society; Director, the CFS-sponsored SPRING Project.

Melanie Klein's remarkable theoretical contributions to psychoanalysis are enduring. The breadth of recent Kleinian intellectual development is thoughtfully presented in Penelope Garvey and Kay Long's edited volume, The Klein Tradition: Lines of Development—Evolution of Theory and Practice over the Decades. This collection stands on the shoulders of volumes of a similar scale from past decades (e.g., Spillius 1988). The authors have approached their aim of showcasing contemporary Kleinian theory and practice by using a global scope that includes authors ranging beyond familiar London Kleinians, as well as applications of Kleinian theory to fields outside of clinical psychoanalysis. The result of their efforts is a rewarding collection of essays. This hefty book includes twenty-seven essays in total: an introduction, twenty-three articles on clinical theory and practice, and four relating to applications of theory to literary and sociological/organizational realms.

Penelope Garvey and Kay Long are consummate scholars of the Kleinian tradition who have come together in a fruitful cross-Atlantic partnership. Garvey is a training analyst of the British Psychoanalytical Society and a coauthor of the New Dictionary of Kleinian Thought (Spillius et al. 2011). Long, a training and supervising analyst at the Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis, teaches and writes about contemporary Kleinian theory and technique.

Garvey and Long open their book with an erudite overview of Klein's work and a consideration of her theoretical and clinical contributions. They emphasize the crucial impetus her development of play technique with children gave to her subsequent theoretical contributions. [[RunningHead]]

Writing as Queer Practice and Pleasure

Steven Botticelli

Steven Botticelli

289 Bleecker Street
New York, NY 10014
Email: srb224@nyu.edu

Steven Botticelli  

I started writing psychoanalytically because I wanted to be seen, and thought I had something to say.

Then, and still, I write to honor and in some way emulate the writers who have moved me: Erica Jong (with her voice); Harold Searles (the delicacy and metaphoric resonance in his renderings of how he spoke to his schizophrenics patients); Edith Wharton (the way she puts a sentence together); Russell Jacoby (polemical power); Edward Said (moral passion); Jonathan Franzen (clause by clause, his ability to take the reader deeper into the minds of his characters); Judith Butler (eloquence and moral sesnsibility); Assata Shakur (raw expression of personal and political rage). A motley personal canon (by no means exhaustive), assembled over a lifetime of promiscuous reading.

Most of my writing proceeds from bringing together an idea from psychoanalysis with something I've picked up from cultural studies, and letting them intermingle. Play around with the ways they might fit together, what parts fit where, what linkages might be made and perhaps extended, and whether something new might be brought into being from the encounter. What might a literary critic's musings on postmodernism suggest about the political implications of contemporary developments in psychoanalysis? What light might be shed by a sociologist's work on the nature of contemporary relationships on my current impasse with a patient? Mix and match, seeing what plugs in where, open to the unexpected connection, the queer thought.

When I make reference here to my identity, I evoke the origins of my writing impulse in the detouring of a natural expressiveness that marked me as queer, targeted me for bullying, and that got shut down by early

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion at the Crossroads: A Contrarian Reading of “on Arrogance”

Jamey Hecht

Jamey Hecht

308 12th Street, Apt. 3L
Brooklyn, NY 11215
Email: jameyhecht@gmail.com

Jamey Hecht  

countertransference, creativity, defense mechanisms, dissociation, oedipus complex, post-traumatic stress disorder, trauma, projective identification, intersubjectivity, inhibition

In “On Arrogance,” Wilfred Bion made a remarkable claim: “the analyst who is treating an apparently neurotic patient must regard a negative therapeutic response together with the appearance of scattered, unrelated references to curiosity, arrogance, and stupidity as evidence that he is in the presence of a psychological catastrophe with which he will have to deal.” However Bion came to believe this, he sought grounds for it in a reading of Oedipus that seriously distorts that tragedy. Since the clinical outcome in “On Arrogance” appears to be impasse, perhaps even iatrogenic injury, the text's value is perhaps best consolidated by historicizing its construction through a psychoanalytic lens. Thus, while “On Arrogance” has been canonized as prescriptive for the treatment of difficult patients, it can instead be read as the illuminating trace of a grievously difficult intrapsychic struggle, a personal reckoning that resulted in Bion's 1958 return to the battlefield at Amiens where he had “died” in the hell of combat forty years earlier. “On Arrogance” thus reflects a prodigious episode of sublimation that proved essential to Bion's lifelong development as a psychoanalytic writer and thinker.

Concerning the Nature of Psychoanalysis: The Persistence of a Paradoxical Discourse. By Gregorio Kohon. London: Routledge, 2019, xxx + 113 pp., $128.00 hardbound, $46.95 paperback.

Samuel Goldberg

Samuel Goldberg

4516 Roxbury Drive
Bethesda, MD 20814
Email: sglmn71@aol.com

Samuel Goldberg   Training and Supervising Analyst, Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis; member, Center for Advanced Psychoanalytic Studies, Princeton, NJ.

The term psychoanalysis may refer to the discipline of analysts, its techniques, findings, and theories. It also may denote the phenomenology that unfolds when two people mutually undertake “a psychoanalysis.” In what follows, I will focus on the latter meaning.

The title of Gregorio Kohon's book invites the question “What is ‘the nature of psychoanalysis’?” The English word “nature” is derived from the Latin “natura,” a translation of the Greek “physis,” derived from the verb “phuo,” meaning to grow, to produce, to generate, to beget, or, as an intransitive, to arise, to spring up (Naddaf 2005). “Physis” thus originally referred to processes of biology, growth, and development. Unsurprisingly, from “physis” also derive our English words “physiology” and “physician,” as well as “physics,” the most comprehensive science, encompassing chemistry and biology.

For Aristotle (350 b.C.), nature most generally “is a source or cause (intrinsic to a thing) of being moved and of being at rest,” as distinguished from other causes of motion or change, such as externally applied violence, or human art. Each thing said to “have a nature … is a substance; for … nature implies a subject in which it inheres” (II.1.192a, 14-18; 33-35). Aristotle posited that something does not yet have its own “nature,” however, until the underlying material of which it is made has received the form that defines it as being that thing. (Thus, for example, calcium becomes bone.) The form is the actualization of what in the material had existed only potentially. Thus, “a thing's nature [is] exhibited in the process of growth by which its nature is attained” (193a, 36; 193b, 7-14). Just as we observe an infant growing through childhood and adolescence, so also in the unfolding phenomenology of a psychoanalysis we observe a natural process of growth by which a person's psychological 1219

[[RunningHead]]

The Journey of Individuation: Experiences of Chinese Patients Treated by Western Psychoanalysts in Online Psychodynamic Psychotherapy

Zhengjia Ren and Zhongyao Xie

Zhongyao Xie

No. 1 Shuanghu Branch Road
Yubei District
Ghongqing 401120
CHINA
Email: renzhengjia@hotmail.com
https://orcid.org/GGGG-GGG1-8499-76GX

Zhongyao Xie  

psychodynamic psychotherapy, online treatment, therapeutic process, cross-cultural treatment

This qualitative study focuses on the process of treatment over the internet from a psychodynamic perspective based on the experiences of seventeen patients in China who underwent online treatment by psychoanalysts based in the United States or other Western nations. The data were collected and analyzed using psychodynamic themes. Seventeen participants involved in online cross-national and cross-cultural psychodynamic treatment were recruited in China. Various themes in regard to the process of online treatment in a cross-national and cross-cultural context emerged. Findings indicate the following: (1) in contrast to traditional relational patterns, psychodynamic treatment provides a new relationship model; (2) psychodynamic treatment creates a space of self-reflection for Chinese patients in terms of their relationships; (3) the therapeutic setting emphasizes clear boundaries, free space, equal relationships, and emotional expression for patients. The results provide new insights into self-examination, which frees patients from the inflexible aspects of traditional relationships, family hierarchies, and rigid cultural values. In addition, psychodynamic treatment in a cross-national and cross-cultural setting not only enables patients to work through their trauma but also empowers them to explore their unique journey of individuation.

[[RunningHead]]

Writing as a Path for Discovery

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

1501 Beacon Street, Apt. 503
Brookline, MA 02446
Email: judy_kantrowitz@hms.harvard.edu

Judy Kantrowitz  

I write to figure out what I think. Both Warren Poland and Glen Gabbard have said the same. But, surely, if someone asked any of us to say what we think about a topic, we'd come up with something relevant. Yet writing is different. How so? For starters, writing allows a slower, more considered reflection on whatever one is contemplating. But as I write this now, I see that, while what I just wrote is true, there's more to it.

When I was an adolescent, I thought I wanted to be a short story writer. As I reflect on this ambition, I wonder why. I was then, and remain, an addictive reader of novels, often long, complex novels where characters develop, evolve, change—not something containable in a short story. Nor did I love reading short stories the way I did novels, because the form didn't allow for such character development. I have a close friend, Edith Perlman, who is a gifted short story writer. Edith transforms her anxieties into transporting tales. Although I did write a few short stories stimulated by adolescent angst, by mid-college I was aware I didn't have the imaginative gift to create compelling narratives. I ask myself, what was it in literature that I really loved? The answer is pretty clear: character—its complexity, development, how people became who they were, how they engaged, changed, how these changes affected them and others.

Well, there's a pretty straight line from that recognition to becoming a psychoanalyst. But for me, as a psychologist, the route at the time was a bit more circuitous. I had to write, but a doctoral dissertation and the first research projects that enabled me to have full analytic training were not writing that deeply engaged me. I wrote to get past hurdles. And then I found I was writing again.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Corresponding Member, Faculty of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Limit of Intimacy and the Intimacy of Limit: Play and its Relation to the Bad Object

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

5 West 86th Street, 11E
New York, NY 10024
Email: stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper  

play, limit, transference, countertransference, paradox, object, containment, holding, erotic, setting

Attachment to the bad object has remained a durable, undertheorized clinical problem. With an extended clinical example, the experience of limit, in both patient and analyst, is examined as part of a dense undercurrent in the relationship, including transference, that gives rise to shifts in understanding the attachment to an unsatisfying internal object. Importantly, the patient's and the analyst's experiences of limit are “in play” during the process of changes in the patient's attachment to the bad object. The relation of patient and analyst to the patient's internal objects, including bad and unsatisfying objects, is where play itself begins. Limit itself is constitutive of play. The analyst's attempt to analyze his own thoughts and experience regarding limits in maintaining an empathic connection to the patient's psychic reality influences the patient's capacity to take in a new part of his or her experience.

The Place Where We Live: Inside, Outside, Playing and Using in Perpetuity

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 8, 1967-1968. Edited by Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016.

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

5 West 86th Street, #11E
New York, NY 10024
Email: stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; faculty, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Among the many brilliant elements of Winnicott's writing is the tapestry of interwoven themes that papers present when taken together. Volume 8 of The Collective Works of Donald Winnicott includes many of his most important theoretical and clinical papers.

There are at least three periods of his work in which monumental changes in theory are introduced. In the first period, summarized by the statement “there is no such thing as an infant” (Winnicott 1941), he is creating an entirely different way of understanding an infant, one that necessarily includes the environment. In the next phase, through introducing us to transitional phenomena and the original me/not-me possession, he is suggesting something in between what is inside and outside, between the intra-and the interpsychic. His final phase of work, the subject of this volume, includes his emphasis on playing as therapeutic action, as well as his major theoretical revision of Freud's and Klein's views on aggression. In this final phase, encapsulated in this volume, it is striking how themes of playing and using the object, as well as his ideas about interpretation and culture, are interwoven in each of the seminal papers.

In the years 1967-1968, Winnicott elaborated his theory of play as central to the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis. He wrote an astounding paper on interpretation in psychoanalysis (Winnicott 1968a) in which he essentially formalized the concept of ontological meanings of interpretation, even as they had been in development during his contribution to transitional phenomena (Winnicott 1951), and as far back as his paper on primitive emotional development (1943). From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Serles of Reviews (Continued)

In this issue we present the ninth essay in an extraordinary series reviewing The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott, brought to us by guest editors Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris. Volume 9 covers the final years of Winnicott's life (1969-1971). In a piece that, like Winnicott's writing, moves the reader with its creativity, Ken Corbett shows us Winnicott's enduring commitment to human growth, even through the process of his own dying. Corbett cites this written prayer of Winnicott's: “Oh God! May I be alive when I die.” Drawing on Winnicott's writing from the period of his “growing into dying,” Corbett suggests that for Winnicott the moon served “as a transitional object to give shape to that paradox.” Hence the evocative title of Corbett's essay: “The Moon of the Magic Casement: Transitional Phenomena, the Moon, and D. W. Winnicott's Death.”

Book Essay: Melanie Klein and Diane Arbus through the Lens of the Relational Image

In this essay, psychoanalyst and photographer Mark Gerald sets out to explore the “relational image” by juxtaposing the lives of Melanie Klein and Diane Arbus. As Gerald defines it, the relational image refers to a “new visual/emotional event created when two mutually vulnerable partners come to see one another in an entirely fresh way.” Through Roger Amos's Portrait of a Life: Melanie Klein and the Artists, Gerald explores the notion of portraiture as a relational creation, emphasizing that “the artist must be able to see the subject from the subject's perspective” and, thus, that the portrait that emerges is the result of a brief collaborative encounter between artist and subject. Turning to the life of the photographer Diane Arbus as depicted by Arthur Lubow in Diane Arbus: Portrait of a Photographer, Gerald reflects on the work of Arbus, who—like Klein—was a pioneering and profoundly influential figure in her field. Arbus's work, Gerald writes, “was damned for its voyeurism and praised 367

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 9

Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

Winnicott, who died in 1971, produced the material in Volume 9 of the Collected Works (1969-1971) in the shadow of his approaching death. After a lifetime of theoretical writing and clinical work, he finally felt liberated to unapologetically embrace his own creativity and imagination. There is a freedom in these late essays to more deeply engage with play itself as the very heart of the therapeutic endeavor. It is through play that personal meanings are generated; it is only through play that we come to know we are alive and authentic.

In his powerful and poetic voice, Ken Corbett extends Winnicott's transitional object concept and shows how it shepherds our terrifying entrance and exit from the human community. Winnicott's musings on the moon allowed him “the living of his dying”—the forging of meaning even as he approached a state of nonbeing that is literally unimaginable. It is the embrace of this kind of paradox that allowed Winnicott to enlarge psychoanalysis, enabling it to engage and assist patients who fear they have not yet begun to exist.

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

Contemporary Conflict Theory: The Journey of a Psychoanalyst. Selected Papers of Sander Abend, M.D. New York: International Psychoanalytic Books, 2018, 445 pp., $35.00 paperback.

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

246 Eliot Street
Chestnut Hill, MA 02467
Email: drfredbusch@gmail.com

Fred Busch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; faculty, Los Angeles Institute for Psychoanalytic Studies.

Sander Abend played an important role in psychoanalysis for over forty years: as editor of Psychoanalytic Quarterly, in his many contributions to the psychoanalytic literature, and through his involvement with psychoanalytic organizations at all levels. A few years before his recent death, he published an excellent selection of his papers. I wrote this review a few months before he died, and the book serves as an eloquent example of the quality of Abend's work. This collection serves as a testament to his ability to consider and incorporate new ideas, while also holding to basic Freudian principles. What I noticed immediately in reading these papers was the clarity of Abend's thinking and writing. This lucidity was a welcome relief from what seems an increasing tendency in the literature to view murky, abstract thinking as a sign of intellectual brilliance.

Steven Tuber   Professor of Psychology, Director of Clinical Training and Program Head, Doctoral Program in Clinical Psychology, City College, CUNY.

If you would listen, that would be best.

—Piggle

It was with a significant amount of both trepidation and excited anticipation that I took on the task of reviewing a book titled Finding the Piggle. Having spent the majority of my adult professional life reading and rereading Winnicott, teaching and reteaching Winnicott to doctoral psychology students, writing and rewriting about him (Tuber 2008), and, more important, loving him as a clinician, a theoretician, and a mensch of the highest order, I felt that being able to approach Corinne Masur's edited volume in an open-minded and open-hearted way would prove difficult indeed. Could Winnicott stand up to the potential criticism of these authors, and could I? Or, conversely, would it be yet another idealization or, worse yet, overly simplistic rendition of “the Great Man”?

Equally drawing me to the task was that I had not reread The Piggle since graduate school, some forty years ago. It had in fact just been published in my second year of graduate school and provided me my first model of who I wanted to be as a clinician, as Winnicott's playfulness commingled with his warmth and depth of humanity in a way I had not yet thought possible as I absorbed Freud and his circle and the new “upstarts” in the field, Kernberg and Kohut.

My first order of business therefore was to reread The Piggle, and there are a number of points I want to make about this great work before tackling Masur and her contributors.

In their eleventh consultation together, Piggle begins by noting that her baby sister can understand things better now with age. Winnicott asks Piggle: “would it be better if I didn't talk,” to which Piggle replies, “If you would listen, that would be best.” Winnicott: “Shall I talk or listen?” Piggle: “Listen!”

This interchange speaks volumes about both therapist and patient. For Winnicott, his readily admitting (by asking the first question) that he often interpreted too much too quickly to Piggle was both humble and 416

“Day's Residues”: One Vertex among Many

Cecilia Taiana

Cecilia Taiana

33 Elmdale Avenue
Ottawa, Ontario K1M 1A1
CANADA
E-mail: cecilia.taiana@carleton.ca

Cecilia Taiana  

dreams, Freudian theory, intersubjectivity, day's residues, metaphors, waking-dream-thought, post-Bionian field theory, transformation in dreaming and play, semiotic index, atmosphere

The post-Bionian paradigm in psychoanalysis invites us to listen to the session as a waking-dream-thought where unconscious-thinking-in progress is continuous. The hypothesis put forward here and illustrated using clinical material is that we can use the notion of day's residues as a metaphor to refer to the incoming narrative of the patient. Whatever the patient brings to the session can be conceived as “day's residues” in that they are potential instigators of waking-dream-thought in the session. This metaphor helps the analyst place brackets around the outside of the session, deconcretizing what apparently are hard facts, so that immediate contact is made to create a shared perspective, possibly producing in this session “food” for the mind. To create the waking-dream-thought of the session, the analyst may consider listening to the incoming narrative as a metaphor. This is not a new or different concept but a particular kind of elaboration on the metaphoric stance taken by psychoanalysts of all stripes; it is an elaboration that expands the ways we can describe the session and narrow the gap between talking about the session and the experience of the session itself.

East Meets West: Introduction to Ren and Xie

Anne Erreich

Anne Erreich

170 West End Avenue, Suite 1S
New York, NY 10023
Email: anne@erreich.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8073-3754

Anne Erreich  

I can't help it, I'm so anal.

—NYC dental hygienist explaining her thoroughness

Could the founder of psychoanalysis have been an Asian?

As interest in psychoanalysis and psychodynamic psychotherapy expands into Asian cultures, and especially since the IPA plans to include this area of the world as a new regional member, we in the West wonder what an Asian population will make of this Western invention. How will Asians, East Asians in particular, embrace our export?

One of the enduring tensions in psychoanalysis, both as a model of mind and as a therapeutic technique, is the extent to which it represents universal aspects of human experience as against particularly Western attitudes claiming to be universal. When I began training, I imagined that I was undertaking the study of psychoanalysis as a repository of universal truths, but my years spent treating and supervising East Asians has convinced me that this is not the case. I have come to believe that the basic assumptions and goals of psychoanalytic theory have a distinctly Western flavor, that they are undergirded by values that are anything but universal.

The following paper, submitted by two Chinese authors, Zhengjia Ren and Zhongyao Xie, takes up this issue, that is, the challenge of introducing a Chinese population to some basic psychoanalytic assumptions: the notion of unconscious processes and content, defenses against strong affects, the importance of an interior life and the capacity to mentalize others, the tension between autonomy and relatedness, notions of authority, and the oedipus complex, especially as these contradict the East Asian emphasis on filial piety. Ren and Xie interview Chinese patients and describe their experience of being treated in psychoanalysis or psychotherapy by Western psychoanalysts.

729

[[RunningHead]]

Setting the Record Straight

Barbara Eisold

Barbara Eisold

353 Central Park West
New York, NY10025
Email: beisold0@gmail.com

Barbara Eisold  

July 2, 2020. In JAPA 68/1 (pp. 131-136), my recent book, Psychodynamic Perspectives on Asylum Seekers and the Asylum-Seeking Process (Routledge, 2019), was reviewed by Steven Botticelli. I very much appreciate having my book reviewed in JAPA and thought the review was, for the most part, very thoughtful and supportive.

However, in reviewing chapter 4, “Central American Women on the Run: Feminicide and Its History,” the reviewer made the following comment: “In the absence of data to support Eisold's implicit contention that such abuse is in fact more prevalent among Central American men than among U.S. men or men generally, this speculation carries an unfortunate (and unintentional) racist tinge” (p. 135).

In fact, contrary to the assertion that I provide no data to support my contention, there are, in the end notes to chapter 4, a number of supportive references (notes 11, 12, and 13). I assume that the reviewer did not read these.

In addition, I would point out that Central American men are not a “race,” but a gender group. Although I may harbor some unconscious prejudices in regard to this gender group, this is quite different from my “speculation” having a “racist tinge.” These days any term reflecting racism has hostile implications, which would not reflect my feelings at all.

Before sending this letter to JAPA I sent it to Dr. Botticelli for his comments. He replied as follows: “Point taken about Central American men and my imputation of racism.” This sentence satisfies my need for him to correct his original assessment. Needing to set the record straight publicly, I am sending this letter.

Barbara Eisold

353 Central Park West

New York, NY10025

Email: beisold0@gmail.com

[[RunningHead]]

Playing with Winnicott: Squiggling Through Therapeutic Consultations

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 10. Therapeutic Consultations in Child Psychiatry. Edited by Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016.

Jack Foehl and Christopher Bonovitz

Christopher Bonovitz

3 Concord Avenue, #5
Cambridge, MA0213
Email: jackfoehl@gmail.com

Christopher Bonovitz   Faculty and Supervising and Training Analyst, William Alanson White Institute; Clinical Associate Professor of Psychology and Clinical Consultant, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; faculty and Supervisor, Mitchell Center for Relational Studies and Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Editor, Psychoanalytic Dialogues and Contemporary Psychoanalysis.

During the final two years of his life, Winnicott worked with a sense of urgency to complete several key projects including this volume, originally published in 1971, just eight months after his death. Throughout his career, Winnicott held a multitude of one- or two-session consultations with children of varying ages. These consultations, or first interviews, held a special significance for him: “I was struck by the frequency with which the children had dreams of me the night before attending” (p. 30).1 They brought “their imaginative equipment” and thus afforded the possibility of an encounter that Winnicott characterized as a “sacred moment” (p. 30). Winnicott discovered to his “amusement” that he was “fitting in with a preconceived notion” of who he was to be for the child, and realized that he had entered a very special role that he would later come to call a “subjective object” (p. 30).

The child brings an illusion of expectation to the setting that meets the reality of the doctor's being there, responding to the child's belief in the possibility of being understood. Winnicott notes that “in this role of subjective object, which rarely outlasts the first or first few interviews, the doctor has a great opportunity for being in touch with the child” (p. 30). It is in this setting that the child can bring and display “the current problem or the emotional conflict or the pattern of strain which obtains at this moment of the client's life” (p. 32).

1All citations in this essay are from the volume under review, CW10, unless otherwise noted.977

L'inconscio e L'ambiente: psicoanalisi e ecologia (The Unconscious and the Environment: Psychoanalysis and Ecology). By Cosimo Schinaia. Rome: Alpes Italia, 2020, 176 pp., €16.00.

Simonetta Diena

Simonetta Diena

Via California 9
20144 Milano
ITALY
Email: simonetta.diena@iol.it

Simonetta Diena   Member, Italian Psychoanalytic Society; Training and Supervising Analyst, International Psychoanalytical Association.

There are books that make you reflect on issues that, no matter how broad the scope of your interest or attention, you may have never paused long enough to examine or discover what you don't know. This is one of those books. For Freud, the term psychoanalysis has three definitions: (1) “a procedure for the investigation of mental processes which are almost inaccessible in any other way; (2) “a method (based upon that investigation) for the treatment of neurotic disorders”; (3) “a collection of psychological information obtained along those lines, which is gradually being accumulated into a new scientific discipline” (1923, p. 235).

The commitment that Cosimo Schinaia has made in recent years has a lot to do with the third meaning, that is, to try to use the psychoanalytic method to understand issues concerning the place where we live. For Schinaia, because of our nomadic nature and the vagaries of history, home is always an imaginary place, more grounded in a phantasmatic and mental landscape than in a physical one. The place we live in has an internal reality. Deterioration of the physical environment that surrounds this internal representation of our place, called home, has an emotional impact on that internal reality. Schinaia examines the complex overlapping of external and internal reality in our psychic reactions to environmental issues.

In relation to Freud's third point, this overlapping of internal and environmental forces converges in a new scientific discipline: an approach to the psychoanalytic dimension aimed at questioning precisely the events we are immersed in but do not duly attend to at the conscious level. As his book clearly demonstrates, such events are more firmly rooted in our unconscious. “Psychic traffic jams” (ingorghi psichici) are defined in the book as moments of jumble between external experience and internal elaborations, in the delicate balance between human beings and the environment.

Cynthia Ezell

Cynthia Ezell

408 Phillips Road
Lebanon, TN 37087

Cynthia Ezell   Private practice, marriage and family therapy; graduate, New Directions in Writing Program, Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis.

Lottie Hart, cradling a cage of rats, gently coos the nursery ballad Lullaby and Good Night to calm the nervous rodents as she transports them to her lab where she will, without remorse or regret, slice off their heads with a tiny guillotine. Four children wait for Lottie at home: an eighteen-year-old daughter, a fourteen-year-old stepdaughter, and two sons, ages five and two. With this introduction, Arlene Heyman, author of Artifact, reveals the competing forces that war inside Lottie: maternal urges, with their compelling and complicated gratifications and responsibilities, and the steely determination to sacrifice what she must in order to pursue a career in science. In another early scene, Lottie is giving her rambunctious young sons a bath while she fantasizes about a letter she will write to the editor of a scientific journal who has rejected her research results. Throughout the book, Lottie careens between her two loves: science and family. Artifact explores the losses and achievements of a middle-class woman growing up in mid-century America who is internally driven toward a life in the sciences.

The critique of Lottie's research is that her findings are simply an artifact of the buffers, the fixatives, and the additive combination of agents she uses to perform her experiments on the salivary glands of rats. She is hoping to discover whether the glands serve an endocrine function. Boys bathed, toweled, and handed off to their father, Lottie's second husband, Jake, she types her reply to the editor. “It should be clear to anyone with experience in this business that all one ever deals with is artifact, and that one's skills in creating and interpreting artifact are largely the measure of one's abilities as a morphologist/scientist” (p. 9).

Arlene Heyman is a scientist herself, a medical doctor and psychoanalyst. Her facility with scientific processes and language makes reading Artifact a sort of primer on the scientific life as experienced by a woman 1023

I welcomed the discovery of Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Intense Involvement in Sports, as I had the issue of Contemporary Psychoanalysis (46/4, 2010) where eight of its twelve papers first appeared. This book appeals to me as a sports fan, though I imagine it can also provide a helpful entry into the mind of a sports fan for those not afflicted with a love of sports.

In keeping with the tone of the book, whose contributors share their experiences as sports fans and participants, I will start with a few scenes from my own love of sports. I spent hours in the driveway of my family home shooting baskets, imagining that I was Walt Frazier, the great shooting guard from my favorite team at that time, the New York Knicks. The clock would be winding down, and in the final seconds I would put up a potential winning shot; if I missed I would have been fouled, and I'd go to the foul line to take my foul shots. Whatever the outcome, I could rewind and play the situation again and again. Reflecting with pleasure on a similar experience in his past, Steven Cooper concludes, “It makes me long for the simplicity of the useless play of sport” (p. 48).

During my adolescence, my room was graced with a poster of John Carlos and Tommie Smith standing on the Olympic medal stand with gloved fists raised in a salute, as a statement of protest and Black pride. Their combination of athletic excellence and righteous anger captured my imagination. Finally, I reflected on my continuing love of collecting 1230

On the Word's Work

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1
Pavia 27100
ITALY
Email: gcivitarese@gmail,com

Giuseppe Civitarese   Training and Supervising Analyst, Italian Psychoanalytic Society and American Psychoanalytic Association.

For me, I guess, writing is rooted in long sittings with Pinocchio when I was a child, which my mother and I took turns reading aloud. Perhaps, on an oedipal level, it has also to do with transgression and reparation (a pair of concepts that, as it happens, bring together our analytic parents). I mean a certain taste for intertextuality, a sort of spontaneous attitude to weave threads coming from different disciplinary fields. Not only, therefore, an urge to cross boundaries, but also to synthesize: to reunite what is separated, to solve a puzzle, to put back together the pieces of a broken vase. It is no coincidence that I titled one on my more personal books Transpositions (Civitarese 2017). I can't stay in enclosures, but I've learned over time that I don't have to worry about my tendency to disperse myself into quite disparate readings and interests. After all, anyone who engages in writing, including writing nonfiction, is obeying an obsession that has a demonic quality. Although it operates in the dark, this internal presence leaves little room for distraction.

What fascinates me about psychoanalytic writing is its more “literary” side, to the extent that it creates worlds. Every author who manages to find a distinct voice expresses her understanding also, and perhaps above all, through style. Freud, Klein, and Winnicott are prime examples of this. Style is not spurious or secondary to logical argumentation. When style happily marries concepts, it completes them in the same way a perception coordinates fluidly with body movements and vice versa. This ineffable (literally: unexpressable in words), elusive aspect also intervenes in the very process of writing. Writing, then, is like struggling with this obscure “force” to bring it to light and thus exorcise its evil power. As with Orpheus and Eurydice, it is to pretend that it is always possible to snatch a glimmer of sense and meaning from the darkness of our ignorance. Julia Kristeva: Perspectives on her Life and Work

Je me voyage: Mémoires (I Travel Myself: Memories). By Julia Kristeva, interviewed by Samuel Dock. Paris: Fayard, 2016, 316 pp.At the Risk of Thinking: An Intellectual Biography of Julia Kristeva. By Alice Jardine. New York: Bloomsbury Academic, xiv + 400 pp., $95.00 hardcover, $26.95 paperback.

Laura Kleinerman

Laura Kleinerman

156 West 86th Street
New York, NY 10024
Email: lkleinerman@gmail.com

Laura Kleinerman   Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research and The Contemporary Freudian Society.

Reviewing these two books, Je me voyage: Mémoires, by Julia Kristeva and Samuel Dock, and At the Risk of Thinking: An Intellectual Biography of Julia Kristeva by Alice Jardine, presents a fascinating challenge for the reviewer. Both books are out of the ordinary conventions in their respective genres. One is an autobiographical dialogue between the psychoanalyst and philosopher Julia Kristeva and a young psychoanalyst, Samuel Dock, who interviews her over several weeks. The other is an intellectual biography of Kristeva written by a former student and close friend, Alice Jardine. Taken separately and together, the two books elaborate, interlace, and reflect the different voices, layers, and directions of Kristeva's life and work. Kristeva is highlighted in both books as a person, as a psychoanalyst, and as a philosopher. The multifaceted identity the two books depict reflects Kristeva's view that one is not a unified ego but rather a “multiverse” of intertwining and multilayered aspects. In these two books, she is portrayed from a range of perspectives: through her own words, through the questions and comments of Dock, and through the eyes of Jardine.

To write an essay on these books is also an exercise in following a double polyphony and a multiverse. Je me voyage was published in 2016. An English translation followed in 2020, included in The Library of Living Philosophers (Volume 36, The Philosophy of Julia Kristeva). Je me voyage consists of conversations with Kristeva based on questions preceded by careful study and preparation by Dock. The interviews begin with biographical details and 763

[[RunningHead]]

Rudnytsky Revives Ferenczi and Severn

Formulated Experiences: Hidden Realities and Emergent Meanings from Shakespeare to Fromm. By Peter Rudnytsky. New York: Routledge, 2019, xiv + 238 pp., $160.00 hardcover, $39.95 paperback. The Discovery of the Self: A Study in Psychological Cure. By Elizabeth Severn. Edited by Peter Rudnytsky. New York: Routledge, 2017, xviv + 149 pp., $182.00 hardcover, $64.95 paperback.

Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

8109 Horseshoe Lane
Potomac, MD 20854
Email: rwmd@comcast.net

Richard Waugaman   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Georgetown University School of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst Emeritus, Washington Baltimore Psychoanalytic Institute.

Peter Rudnytsky's latest book is a gem, offering us his incisive scholarship on a broad range of fascinating topics. Although most of its nine chapters are previously published, it is valuable to have them more accessible here. The chapters range widely, from Shakespeare and Milton to Freud, Groddeck, Ferenczi, Severn, and Fromm. In discussing the neglected contributions of Georg Groddeck, Rudnytsky quotes his observation that “truth is always ambivalent, both sides are true” (p. 162). Rudnytsky adds, with respect to Groddeck and Freud, that “what is required is a genuinely dialectical perspective that synthesizes” them both (p. 162). We always benefit from a dialectical synthesis of multiple perspectives, though, sadly, too often we lapse instead into unproductive debates involving false binaries.

Rudnytsky locates his “belated discovery” of the works of Erich Fromm as central to this book (p. 1). He credits Adrienne Harris with galvanizing his interest in Fromm—specifically, Fromm's critique of authoritarianism. He uses that critique to launch some trenchant attacks on the Orwellian Donald Trump, who, he contends, “thinks the opposite of what is true” (p. 98), thus trying to foist doublethink on our country. Rudnytsky's adulation of Fromm may lead him to overemphasize Freud's authoritarian tendencies, as the author occasionally lapses into the rhetoric of debate, which is poorly suited to coping with complexity. Sybil Houlding   Faculty and Dean, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

“Dear Candidate, As a first-year candidate, I remember attending the initial meeting of what was to become our local candidate organization. We met at another candidate's home, and I wondered why we were getting together there and not at the Institute where all the other meetings were held. As we began, I sensed, though it was not said directly, that this gathering was not one that some senior member of the Institute would approve of” (p. xxii).

So writes Fred Busch in the introduction to this collection of letters, in which senior analysts invited by Busch each write a letter to today's candidates in which they recall their own training and offer suggestions and advice to candidates in training today. This book both marks and embodies the changes in our profession. No longer is the analyst only a blank screen, available for the projection of fantasies; the analyst is now a real person, whose thoughts can be known. Busch has assembled forty-three letters, solicited from senior analysts practicing in the three regions of the International Psychoanalytical Association: Latin America, Europe, and North America. The cultural differences in institutes reflected in these letters is one of the many pleasures of this book; they serve to introduce the reader, and particularly the candidate for whom this volume is intended, to an international overview of our profession. Missing, however, are any of the well-known senior analysts of color practicing in the United States: Beverly Stoute, Anton Hart, and Dionne Powell, to name a few, all of whom have recently contributed prominently to The American 795

Dear Candidate: Analysts from Around the World Offer Personal Reflections on Psychoanalytic Training, Education, and the Profession. Edited by Fred Busch. New York: Routledge, 2021, xxiv + 170 pp., $31.95 paperback.

Stefany Fortin

Stefany Fortin

110 East 30th Street
New York, NY 10016
Email: fortinstefany@gmail.com

Stefany Fortin   Second-year psychoanalytic candidate, Psychoanalytic Association of New York (PANY).

As if clearing a path for those of us entering the field, the seasoned analysts who contributed to this book reveal the professional and personal twists and turns that led them to choose the impossible profession. They also share what was valuable in their training and what was not, and the cultural and political changes they have witnessed in the profession over the course of their careers. Most contributors address not only the inner pilgrimage one makes during training—understanding oneself through personal analysis, finding and learning a congenial theoretical approach— but also the world around them, citing the relationships they have forged, at their institutes and beyond, as invaluable in building and sustaining their analytic identities. The contributors seem to agree that the years spent at a training institute are but one of many chapters in the lifelong journey of becoming an analyst. Their consensus on this point diffuses some of the pressure one might feel about the high-stake years of candidacy. For those of us who have begun training remotely, as the pandemic kept us away from the classroom and the office—and to those considering training as we slowly find our way back into the world—the insights offered in Dear Candidate can help illuminate what really matters when it comes to becoming an analyst.

I chose my training institute online; interviewed for candidacy on Zoom; started taking my seminars remotely; and recently started seeing my first analytic patient at a distance. I wonder how this strange beginning will shape my growth as an analyst. Knowing that the quality of the earliest moments in mother-infant attachment are crucial to optimal child development, I have wondered if taking my first “analytic steps” in cyberspace might alter my gait as an analyst in the “real” world. Does the convenience of logging in and out of my training analysis by the click of a button permit a lack of commitment, or is it actually making this feasible for me? What of the conundrum of making puzzles with my toddler on the very couch I recline on to put together the puzzle of my psychic life? Owen Lewis   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons; faculty, Division of Narrative Medicine, Department of Medical Humanities and Ethics.

Janet Malcolm (1977) called psychoanalysis the “impossible profession.” If the practice of the profession is indeed “impossible,” effective and persuasive writing about it—writing that deeply conveys the analysand's daily struggles in an analysis—is beyond impossible. Most accounts are either emotionally laden or formulated with an emotionless clinical pseudo-objectivity. In Slow Fuse of the Possible: A Memoir of Poetry and Psychoanalysis, Kate Daniels avoids these extremes and accomplishes a beautiful and persuasive, almost impossible to write, memoir.

A book depends above all on great writing, and Kate Daniels, a poet, knows how to craft words. Sentences flow with balance and grace, surprise and wit. Maureen Katz   Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; member, Holmes Commission for Racial Equality, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Fifty years ago, after hiking in the Sinai Peninsula for a month, we came upon a blackberry bush—species rubes sanctus—at the foot of the biblical Mt. Sinai. It was enclosed ceremoniously in iron fencing and stone. The wilderness guide with whom we'd been traveling explained this was in fact The Burning Bush, “The One” in which God revealed himself to Moses before giving him the Ten Commandments.

I myself had spent the better part of the spring that year pulling blackberry bushes from the yards of homes and trying to eradicate their relentless spread in my job as a landscaper. I knew from the cuts and scars on my hands that you can pull them out, poison and, yes, burn them, and they will nonetheless survive. Of course, this would be The Burning Bush.

I recall this moment while reading Sally Weintrobe's tour de force, Psychological Roots of the Climate Crisis: Neoliberal Exceptionalism and the Culture of Uncare. The roots she painstakingly describes are deep and intertwined and must be understood if any social movement is to make a successful ethical demand for a livable Earth. Weintrobe's profound understanding of states of mind and her expansion of psychoanalytic concepts—which she integrates with an understanding of social, economic, and ideological forces—are singular in this work. Weintrobe fuses theoretical and clinical psychoanalytic acumen in a major contribution to a theory of mind and how that theory might bear on our collective survival.

We are all negatively affected by the climate crisis, in one way or another. Until now, the psychological community's approach to helping patients handle struggles with everything from fear to grief to guilt has mainly been providing insights and tools for living with and adapting to the crisis. In most cases, it's assumed that man-made carbon emissions are at the root of the devastating effects on the planet we're already witnessing, and that individuals have little to no control over systemic causes. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Extremes of Contemporary Violence: From a State of Paralysis to the Transformation of Trauma

Rajaa Stitou

Rajaa Stitou

1 Boulevard Pasteur
34 000 Montpellier
France
Email: rajaa.stitou@univ-montp3.fr

Rajaa Stitou  

Freudian theory, trauma, Lacanian theory, extremism, ethnopsychiatry, culturalism, transformative processes, linguistic difference

Psychoanalysis is today faced, clinically and conceptually, with extremes of violence and forced exile, creating a need to rethink clinical work with patients whose life has been marked by disaster. In work with foreign patients, difficulty in speaking about trauma is sometimes attributed to a linguistic barrier; however, the experience of terror can in any situation whatever be expressed only with difficulty. Cultural differences must not be ignored, but the clinician must find a way to facilitate speech in a subject experiencing a state of dread. Speaking cannot be forced. Offering a presence in which the analyst accepts being the recipient of what is unbearable and recognizes the subject's current state of chaos is essential in establishing or mobilizing transference in work with patients subject to unlinking. This can initiate a transformation of the trauma, as is shown in two clinical vignettes of refugee patients in France who were victims of terror in their countries of origin. The approach discussed here differs from the earlier ethnopsychiatric method, which would reduce these others to exotic envelopes of their culture, independent of their subjectivity.

responseSet.authorMast: Anne Adelman,  Jennifer Stuart and  Rachel Boué Widawsky
responseSet.authorCitation: Adelman, A., Stuart, J. & Widawsky, R. B.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3372-3750

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2403-0549

Rachel Widawsky  

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Series of Reviews (Continued)

In our final issue of 2022, we offer a set of reviews and essays that we hope will have something of interest for everyone. We begin with an essay by the child and adolescent psychoanalyst Judith Yanof on Volume 11 of Winnicott's Collected Works, which contains two major pieces, The Piggle and Human Nature. Yanof offers an in-depth reflection on Winnicott's use of interpretation and play, with a focus on his extraordinary work with the child he and her parents called “the Piggle.”

If Thou Follow Thy Star

In an essay on Mitchell Wilson's The Analyst's Desire: The Ethical Foundation of Clinical Practice, Giuseppe Civitarese explores Wilson's views of psychoanalytic principles through the lens of desire and the ethical practice of psychoanalysis. According to Civitarese, “Wilson's prose expresses the feeling of a marvelous enduring vitality of psychoanalysis and the awareness of its irreducible strength (but also fragility) in saying something true about the essence of what is human.” With his thorough and insightful reading of Wilson's work, Civitarese brings the reader into conversation with the author, as well as with Lacan, Bion, and others who have considered deeply the analyst's ethical stance and her attitude toward her own desire. As Civitarese writes, “Wilson gives a demonstration of what the ethics of psychoanalysis means. He does not regard scientific debate as an irrelevant, merely scholastic or academic activity… . So for him (and I share his view) it is not true that anything goes.” In his deep reading of Wilson's book, Civitarese invites the reader to consider the significance of “radically assum[ing] responsibility for his or her own desire.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

JAPA Prize Announcements

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

♦ JAPA Prize ♦

The $1000 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

♦ JAPA New Author Prize ♦

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

JAPA Prizes for 2022

Dorothy Evans Holmes, Beverly J. Stoute, Alan Bass, Jyoti M. Rao, Peter Loewenberg, and Donald Moss have been awarded the 2021 JAPA Prize for their contributions to the JAPA issue “Psychoanalysis, Race, and Racism” (69/2, pp. 237-380).

Special Mention: Peter Goldberg for “Eating Your Cake and Having It” (69/4, pp. 789-806).

John Dall'Aglio has been awarded the 2021 JAPA New Author Prize for his articles “Sex and Prediction Error, Parts 1-3” (69/4, pp. 693-765).

responseSet.title: Conundrums and Predicaments in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis: The Clinical Moments Project. Edited by Richard Tuch and Lynn S. Kuttnauer. New York: Routledge, 2018, xxiv + 292 pp., $128.00 hardcover, $39.96 paperback.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Conundrums and Predicaments in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis: The Clinical Moments Project. Edited by Richard Tuch and Lynn S. Kuttnauer. New York: Routledge, 2018, xxiv + 292 pp., $128.00 hardcover, $39.96 paperback.

David Goldenberg

David Goldenberg

35 East 85th Street
New York, NY 10028
Email: davidgoldenbergmd@gmail.com

David Goldenberg   Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College, New York Presbyterian Hospital; faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Analysts share an appetite for the details of exemplary clinical work and lively discussion and conflict about the technicalities of working out knotty problems. Conundrums and Predicaments in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis: The Clinical Moments Project, edited by Richard Tuch 219 and Lynn Kuttnauer, presents us with a dozen knots, places where a psychodynamic clinician has reached a dilemma in the treatment. With twenty-five separate commentaries here, plus the presenting therapists’ solutions, we are offered a bounteous buffet of insight and clinical skill. Twelve analysts generously present work that reached a point of problem. Each case is introduced thoughtfully by the editors, outlining the nature of the work and the dilemma. Well-known, eminent analysts provide commentary specific to the clinical situation at hand. In each of the twelve different treatments, the problem arises when the therapist/analyst reaches an impasse, has a question, and doesn't know what to do, feel, or think. This format (presentation and commentary, followed by the analyst's solution) invites the reader to consider his or her own potential experience in that situation: What would I feel, think, say, or “do”—how would I ultimately act in this specific situation?

In my experience, students of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy nearly always appreciate examples of work by more experienced clinicians. Clinicians’ generosity with their own process and insights has led to significant contributions to the field, stimulating fruitful discussion and new perspectives. As a standard-issue psychoanalyst, I value, and find useful, insights from a variety of sources. I suspect most readers will approach the book as I did, seeking help with their own clinical work. Yet while the cases—all readable and well-rendered—are interesting and the dilemmas relatable, I found the book most helpful as a guide to supervision.

As noted in the very useful introduction, “This book is an outgrowth of the Clinical Moments Program that began in Michigan under the tutelage of Dr. Marvin Margolis” (p. 1). In the project, a diverse, community-based psychoanalytic study group, clinical material leading to a “dicey” situation would be presented, followed by facilitated discussion: a sort of group supervision. The twelve cases in the book were first presented in Michigan or California (L.A.), and the group supervision has been replaced by invited commentators.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentaries on Solms's “Revision of Drive Theory”: Editor's Note

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8681-136X

Mitchell Wilson  

Mark Solms opens his paper “Revision of Drive Theory” (JAPA 69/6, 2021) thus: “Psychoanalytic drive theory is in need of fundamental revision. Many analysts today have not only lost confidence in Freud's classical conception of the drives … but have abandoned drive theory altogether, thereby conflating Freudian drive theory with drive theory per se. The price we pay for this is too great; rather than abandon drive theory, we must revise it” (p. 1033).

Solms's paper is part of a far larger project in which he attempts to bring Freudian concepts and models of mind into meaningful conversation with findings from contemporary neuroscience. We had hoped to publish commentaries on his paper at the time of its publication; a variety of factors prevented this. What follows are two commentaries, by Alan Sugarman and Paul Schwartz, and a response to them by Solms.

Orcid iD

Mitchell Wilson https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8681-136X

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu

responseSet.title: Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 11
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 11

Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6638@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

Judith Yanof's engaged and insightful review of Volume 11 is focused on Winnicott's account of his work with a young patient, nicknamed Piggle by her parents, and also called by her name, Gabrielle. She lives in this review—as in Winnicott's essay—with this double identity, playing and suffering. Precocity and trouble interweave in this clinical report for, as Yanof cogently notes, Winnicott is also engaged in his use of conflict and creative engagement with the clinical practices of play and interpretation. At this final stage in Winnicott's career, play is no longer simply a window into the child's unconscious but has become the very engine of therapeutic action, the generator of experiences that allow the child to feel alive, known, and present. Yet Yanof notes that paradoxically, at the beginning of this treatment, Winnicott occasionally offers Kleinian-tinged “deep” interpretations, which overwhelm his young patient. This form of interpretation fades as the treatment progresses and Gabrielle/Piggle and Winnicott learn to play together. Perhaps the nature of play is different for each child, such that even Winnicott himself must learn how to play with this particular child. As Winnicott slowly relaxes into the ease of play, his anxious need to interpret dissipates. In one of his concluding works, Winnicott shows us how the ability of patient and analyst to learn, over time, how to play with each other lies at the very heart of the therapeutic endeavor. Even here, close to the end of Winnicott's career and life, he is still functioning as outsider, as outlier, as maverick.

Email: pb6638@gmail.com

Palo Alto, CA 94306-4551
Email: nmoshe@gmail.com

Nathan Szajnberg   Former Freud Professor, The Hebrew University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Israel Psychoanalytic Society.

This review aims for clarity. Clarity about emotions and emotions only.

Why is clarity about “emotions” necessary? An entire field of neuroscience/neuropsychoanalysis, pioneered by Panksepp, Damasio, Solms, Yovell, and others (Solms 2019), has been exploring “affective neuroscience,” which they have called the study of “emotions,” or “feelings.” But “feelings” include experiences such as my fingertips hitting this keyboard, or an eructation, which presumably are not included in affective neuroscience. And the “emotions” they describe—here's the rub—are not those outlined by Darwin in 1872, and certainly not what emotion researchers have studied since the mid-1960s. “Emotions” for these researchers are discrete phenomena that while challenging to articulate are known to all. Darwin in The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals (1872) described six basic emotions: happiness, sadness, anger, disgust, surprise, and fear. Here I will review Erika Rosenberg and Paul Ekman's update on the empirical basis for the facial expression of these emotions, even as Ekman (2003) has laid out nonfacial expressions of emotions in an earlier book (as for shame, awe, and schadenfreude).

So what do emotion researchers mean by emotions, and how does this help psychoanalysts in clinical work? This new book helps answer these questions.

The book's subtitle further defines the area of study: Basic and Applied Studies of Spontaneous Expression Using the Facial Action Coding System 591
