responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Frontiers or New Countries? A Survey Assessing Covid, Telehealth, and Innovation in Psychoanalysis

Karl Stukenberg , Adam Moriwaki and Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher

3800 Victory Parkway
Cincinnati, OH 45207
Email: stukenb@xavier.edu

Charles Fisher  

telehealth, psychoanalysis, technique, survey, pandemic

A wide-ranging survey focusing on the experiences of analysts providing telehealth treatment approximately six months into the pandemic was sent to one hundred randomly selected APsaA graduate analysts, one hundred randomly selected APsaA candidate analysts, and then to everyone on the APsaA members’ Listserv. Presented both open-ended and closed-ended questions, analysts were invited to quantify and reflect on their experience. Responding analysts in each of the three groups described similar experiences. Each group noted that they have, overall, moved from a neutral to a modestly positive acceptance of telehealth as a means of delivering psychoanalysis. In describing the frame and process of telehealth, the analysts reported wide differences in patient response to the change in format, including positive, negative, and highly specific shifts in the treatment process attributable to the change in format. Telehealth, it appears, is a distinct space in which psychoanalysis can be conducted. In general, respondents minimized the impact of the pandemic itself on the analytic process, though the process may have been more impacted by the pandemic than they were reporting. All three groups almost unanimously reported interest in exploring innovative psychoanalytic techniques.

[[RunningHead]]

JAPA Best Paper Awards 2021

What is Psychoanalysis For?

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu

Mitchell Wilson  

Psychoanalysis is for challenging—disrupting—our most passionately held beliefs and unconsciously taken positions for the purposes of greater freedom to think, choose, and converse with ourselves and others. As analysts we intervene in ways we hope further these aims. We traffic in the most human of domains—our private worlds of desire, aspiration, and fantasy, of trauma, pain, and regret. We are especially alert to moments of impasse—resistance—in ourselves and in those with whom we work as we strive for something closer to truth—the unthought or the unthinkable. We know that once thinkable and perhaps thought, our bulwark of resistance has yielded ever so slightly. New engagements become possible, and what we once struggled to see we can now confront with less fear and greater clarity.

Another way to answer the question “What is psychoanalysis for?” is: to make a difference. The difference we wish to make is at the level of being, at the level of the very existence of the human person. Every once in a while a former patient will say “Analysis saved my life.” It happens; of this there is no question. Through psychoanalysis it is possible to make a difference at the level of being.

Psychoanalytic writing ought to make a difference in this same sense—that is, intervene in a productively disruptive way, and thereby possibly change lives. Those of us who read the literature and contribute to it know that much of the time analytic writing does not have these effects. Often analytic writing, including that found in JAPA, adds a little something to our understanding of this or that theoretical or clinical issue, in the fashion of normal science in the Kuhnian sense. This kind of writing is familiar and reassuring, as it essentially reinforces the status quo ante, common opinion, doxa. Further, it reinforces our collective identity as psychoanalysts, in that we recognize ourselves in at least some of what we read.

Yet we know that the reinforcing of identity—the strengthening of the ego—often cuts both ways, as strength can become rigidity, and identity can be shot through with reassuring ideology. If we keep in mind the 825

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, Apt. 1BB

Jay Greenberg   Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Institute for Psychiatry, Psychoanalysis, and Psychology; former Editor, Psychoanalytic Quarterly; former Editor for North America, International Journal of Psychoanalysis.

Choosing a framework that can adequately celebrate the hundredth anniversary of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis must have been a daunting task. Not only has the Journal published more than ten thousand articles over the course of its history, but tectonic changes in both psychoanalysis and the broader culture have shifted our discourse profoundly, challenging even the possibility of finding or creating any kind of thematic unity.

Faced with this, Dana Birksted-Breen, the Journal's editor for the past fifteen years and editor of Translation/Transformation: 100 Years of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, came up with an inspired choice.

“Translation,” Birksted-Breen's chosen theme, was at the heart of Freud's most important goals for the discipline he created; in at least two very different ways it shaped the history of psychoanalysis and of the Journal. First, translation was central to Freud's clinical project; it is one way of describing what happens when we make the unconscious conscious. At its origins the goal of psychoanalysis was to make it possible for us to translate ourselves to ourselves.

Second, translation (in the more everyday use of the word) was necessary to Freud's project of changing the world, of bringing about the third Copernican Revolution (Freud 1916-1917, p. 285). If the world was to be changed, psychoanalytic ideas had to be disseminated beyond the small group of German speakers who had gathered around Freud from the beginning. The dissemination of ideas across cultures—and, crucially, the 401

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 10

Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

In this magical review essay, John Foehl and Christopher Bonovitz, two deeply engaged clinicians, bring the clinical sensibilities of Winnicott's work with children into full daylight. Why magical? The authors are so wonderfully reactive to the clinical Winnicott they find in these pages, the man speaking both to children and about children. Perhaps magic may be the only word that can convey the mystery of the evolving experience of knowing and being generated by Winnicott and his child patients engaging in forms of play with no expectations of what it might see or find. It made us realize that in so much of the Collected Works Winnicott is talking to adults, peers, and colleagues and teaching us in his inimitable manner, but in this volume we witness twenty-one different brief consultations with children. We listen to Winnicott at work, speaking to children and then about children to his reader. It seems magical to have this unexpected moment of supervision and witnessing; to be given this experiential counterpart to Winnicott's earlier theoretical work.

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

[[RunningHead]]

As I Write

Fred Griffin

Fred Griffin

5324 Wenonah Drive
Dallas, TX 75209
Email: fredgriffinmd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8868-8900

Fred Griffin  

To ask a writer why he writes is to ask a very personal question. If he actually knows something of why, the answer becomes an even more intimate one.

I write because I am afraid I will not be able to write.

I could say that I want the freedom I experience in writing. The solitude it provides. The way I have conversations with myself and with others I will never meet. The community of writers I feel recognized by and a part of. The opportunity to contribute to the literature of psychoanalysis. All of this is true. But it evades a fundamental truth: I have a dread of not being able to return to the unique state of consciousness I find when writing.

It has taken me a long time to know what I mean by these comments. Twenty-five years ago, during a painful period of my life created by a trauma, I was made all too well acquainted with feelings of helplessness and of a sense of alienation. For a time, I lost the capacity to view myself and others in my world as complex characters. I no longer saw in colors or felt a sense of the continuity of past, present, future. I saw in black and white, two-dimensionally. Fortunately, this was a temporary state of mind. But my memory remains of it as a sort of locked-in experience, one I did not have the capacity to work myself out of at the time.

I discovered during this period that reading works of literary fiction provided a kind of preserve: a protected space in which I felt less alone, less cut off from myself, more alive. I could again appreciate complexity, imagine my universe in colors. Here I felt less defined by a single moment in time. This benefit was temporary. So I kept reading. The more I read, the more I wanted to write. I began writing as a means to understand how this process had worked for me (Griffin 2005, 2016). Along the way, I wrote about my painful experience as a piece of autobiographical fiction

Training and Supervising Analyst, Dallas Psychoanalytic Center; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UT Southwestern Medical School.

Sigmund Freud-Martha Bernays Letters

Sigmund Freud-Martha Bernays: Die Brautbriefe, Band 4: Spuren von unserer komplizierten Existenz (Courtship Letters, Vol. 4: Traces of Our Complicated Existence), September 1884-August 1885. Rita Teusch

Rita Teusch

144-A Mt. Auburn Street
Cambridge, MA 12138

Rita Teusch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Instructor in Psychiatry (Psychology), part-time, Cambridge Health Alliance, Harvard Medical School.

This fascinating book of love letters is the fourth in a series of eventually five volumes containing the unabridged personal letters (in total 1,539) written by Sigmund Freud (1856-1938) and his wife-to-be Martha Bernays (1861-1951) during their four years of engagement (1882-1886), during which Freud lived and worked in Vienna and Bernays lived with her mother and her sister Minna (four years her junior) in Wandsbek, a borough of Hamburg. These volumes offer a unique window into Freud's personal and professional development as a young man, and introduce us to Martha Bernays, whose intelligence, writing skills, astonishing independence, maturity, equanimity, and love for Freud become apparent in these letters. Bernays was part of an esteemed Jewish intellectual family; self-educated, she was an ardent reader of European and American novels, poetry, and plays; she enjoyed opera and the fine arts, and maintained a lively interest in the political and social situation of her time. Reviews of the previous three volumes in this remarkable series can be found in JAPA (Maetzener 2012; Teusch 2014, 2017). These reviews include detailed presentations and discussions of the impressive editorial work and commentary by the editors, who explain the complex history of these publications, provide information about their work on this voluminous project, and describe the research that went into elaborate footnotes providing additional information about every place Freud or Bernays visited, every person they mentioned or interacted with in these letters, every book they talked about and read, the operas they heard, the 553

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Editor

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu

Mitchell Wilson  

The role of a psychoanalytic journal is at least twofold: to reflect the state of the field and to contribute to leading the field in new directions. The former is the familiar project, both necessary and reassuring, and one that JAPA takes seriously. The latter is far less clear, because publishing work in areas that some find only tangentially related to psychoanalysis calls into question the boundaries of our knowledge base; for others it is precisely those boundaries that require ongoing reconsideration. Over the past three years at JAPA we have published a wide range of articles and essays that together reflect important work by analysts and analytically oriented therapists and researchers, and also, at times, push the field in new directions. These have included (this is by no means an exhaustive list) methodologically rigorous quantitative studies; a variety of innovative clinical writings; papers on the ongoing effects of the Covid pandemic on training, practice, and clinical theory; Winnicott in America; Lacanian neuropsychoanalysis; race and racism in psychoanalysis; the psychoanalytic body; and essays and memoirs from some of our leading analysts.

As Editor I firmly believe that our psychoanalytic purview extends beyond the clinic, and that the clinic is embedded in broader social and symbolic forces that cannot be ignored. That this emphasis raises important and complex questions, not only about the knowledge base of psychoanalysis, but also regarding the nature and status of boundaries themselves (inside/outside, surface/depth, and the like), is to the good, as we continue the ongoing project of both refinding and redefining psychoanalysis. Different forms of writing—in addition to the conventional psychoanalytic paper—also contribute to this effort, including interdisciplinary work, papers that review the state of the art of an important topic, and essays that explore a conceptual theme or problem.

For many decades, JAPA has benefited from manuscript submissions from a wide range of sources, and also from invited papers, plenaries, commentaries, and essays. Manuscript submissions tend to reflect the broader state of the field at any given moment; they are vetted for the quality of their 7

responseSet.title: Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 8
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Winnicott's Collected Works, Volume 8

Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

It is fitting that this essay by Stephen Cooper, one of the most innovative North American theorists—who draws on Winnicott for his own work on creativity—should explore this particular volume of the Collected Works, which he rightly characterizes as documenting the apex of Winnicott's extraordinary career as clinician and theorist. There are many things to consider about Volume 8; along with Cooper, we marvel at the astonishing power of the writing and theorizing accomplished in a two-year period.

As Cooper clearly notes, this volume has several “astounding” papers, breaking new ground in our understanding of the ontological functions of interpretation while simultaneously opening new vistas in our conceptualization of aggression. Interpretation is no longer fostered by the analyst's wish to explicate the unconscious but instead serves to establish a site for the play of thoughts, feelings, and language where patients can encounter who they truly are and who they are truly becoming.

Cooper identifies Winnicott both as creative theorist and as teacher; both roles shape the tone and emotional tenor of Winnicott's writing, its accessibility, and its modesty. As Cooper astutely notes, often in the work of Winnicott his tone and style can somewhat mask his powerful divergence from classical theory. Cooper helps us notice that Winnicott took us way beyond the world of object relations into a world of action and setting: where and how. Even if Winnicott's modesty was in part defensive, this way of writing sustains his lively engagement with Freud, Klein, and Bion. His work generously reframes and illuminates the ideas of these earlier pioneers, allowing them to remain essential sources of Winnicott's imagination.

Email: pb6338@gmail.com

Mariana Rodriguez   Psychoanalytic group psychotherapist, Maison Saint-Jacques.

Marilú Pelento (1932-2014), a renowned Argentinian child and adolescent psychoanalyst, is remembered for her work on loss and bereavement linked to abductions and disappearances during Argentina's Dirty War, as well as for her work with groups of marginalized youth. Apart from her clinical work, Pelento was an active participant in her country's human rights movement, with a particular interest in the return of children removed from their families during the dictatorship.

Although Pelento wrote many articles throughout her career and was sought after as a lecturer, she never wrote a book. Marilú Pelento, Psychoanalyst of Our Time is a posthumous collection of articles and lectures, many of them unpublished in her lifetime, compiled by three of Pelento's colleagues and longtime friends: Janine Puget, Julia Braun, and Marité Cena.

Each of the book's chapters is devoted to a different topic. Subjects range from the effects of violence and state terrorism on children and families, to tattoos and body modification techniques, to the effects of cultural changes on the practice of psychoanalysis. Although the texts are all stimulating and thought-provoking, the reader is often left wishing that the topic at hand had been further developed. Pelento's work is suggestive and evocative rather than conclusive; she avoids synthesis and resists a strict order of discourse. Indeed, to comment on Pelento's work, I feel compelled to abandon my expectations of synthesis and to proceed in a manner that is unavoidably partial, leaving aside a number of matters that would otherwise merit attention.

16 Robin Road

Cuneyt Iscan   Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, University of Massachusetts Medical School, Department of Psychiatry.

Is the field of philosophy relevant for psychoanalysis, and, if it is, what does it have to contribute to the field? By the same token, can it be argued that psychoanalysis is relevant for philosophy, and, if it is, what does it have to contribute to that field? Jerome Wakefield's meticulous and brilliant work, powered by his scholarly knowledge in the areas of philosophy and psychoanalysis, is displayed in this book and is welcomed in this context.

Wakefield, in his first chapter, poses the question “Should Freud be taken seriously as a philosopher of mind?” In the three hundred sixty pages that follow, he takes us on an informative, stimulating, highly enriching, and fascinating journey into the complex relationship between philosophy and psychoanalysis. Carefully, systemically, and patiently, Wakefield delineates a certain thread and develops a strong argument that Freud broke ground that philosophy of mind has trod in irreversible ways.

Wakefield responds affirmatively to the question he poses. Freud, he argues, should be viewed as a philosopher, a philosopher of mind: “scattered throughout Freud's writings there is a systematic and—even when judged by current philosophical standards—rather sophisticated and subtle philosophical argument about the nature of the mental.” The argument he reconstructs “reveals Freud as a prescient and important philosopher of mind who made a seminal and underappreciated contribution when judged by the concerns that dominate philosophy of mind in the analytic tradition today” (p. 2).

Alfred Tauber lays out the birth pains of positivism and scientism in the nineteenth century and Freud's own experience of this struggle in “Freud's Philosophical Path” (2009) and later in “Freud: The Reluctant Philosopher” (2010). In the former, Tauber traces the historical origins of 215

“A focus on the match between patient and analyst places attention on the dynamic effect of the interaction of character and conflict of both participants on the process that evolves between them” (p. 29).

This first sentence of Judy Kantrowitz's first paper on patient-analyst match—written in 1986 and included in this book as chapter 1—was a radical statement in the context of American ego psychology. Like so many of Kantrowitz's sentences, it is carefully crafted, simply stated, and full of meaning. It introduces a new and distinct voice in American psychoanalysis, one clinically astute while also pursuing empirical evidence for what actually works in clinical psychoanalysis, regardless of the practitioner's theoretical preference. She was ahead of her time.

Response

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place, 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
Email: donaldmoss@mindspring.com

Donald Moss  

December 19, 2021. I was honored to have been included with Dorothy Holmes, Beverly Stoute, Jyoti Rao, Alan Bass, and Peter Loewenberg in JAPANS extraordinary issue “Psychoanalysis, Race, and Racism.”

“On Having Whiteness” has generated a wide range of readings, from the warmly appreciative to the murderously inflamed. I was unprepared for the wide-reaching tumult. I had thought of the paper as a straightforward effort at reworking some familiar ideas. But, just as in the clinical situation, in the publishing situation the moment you let your words go you lose control of their meanings. Your words come back to you, their meanings transformed by the listener or reader, and you then have the opportunity to listen, to learn, and to go at it again, influenced this time by your experiences of surprise.

I very much appreciate the opportunity to “go at it again” here, to respond to letters from three readers and hopefully to deepen our psychoanalytic and personal capacities to address the malign and enduring presence of the impulses, ideas, and actions structuring the ideology of white supremacy.

Leon Hoffman expresses deep concern about my use of the words “parasite” and “parasitic.” He reminds us that, for centuries, these words have been used in murderous anti-Semitic slurs characterizing an entire people, positioning them as appropriate for elimination. I was stunned by his reading. Had I inadvertently manufactured a Trojan horse carrying an anti-Semitic slur?

Hoffman concludes his letter by referring to “Moss's use of the term ‘White Parasitism.’” Had I written that, I would absolutely agree with him that I was using the word to slur a category of person. But, in fact, I had not used that phrase. What seems to have happened is a kind of slippage of meaning. I used the words “parasite” and “parasitic” as a way of thinking metaphorically about a process, a mode of transmission by which white people can become infiltrated with white racist impulses and ideas. I was thinking about and trying to imagine the vector through which notions of white supremacy enter white people. How can we best name that vector: an agent that enters us, takes residence inside, diminishes us, feeds on our liveliness—an agent that we can carry and transmit, an agent with the power to take control, to turn us into its unwitting hosts, vehicles for its replication? I called the vector “parasitic-like”; I called the people it infiltrates “susceptible”: “I will focus,” I wrote, “on Whiteness as a condition one first acquires and then has—a malignant, parasitic-like 360

Melanie Klein and Diane Arbus Through the Lens of the Relational Image

Portrait of a Life: Melanie Klein and the Artists. By Roger Amos. London: Phoenix Publishing, 2019, xviv + 133 pp., $35.00 hardcover. Diane Arbus: Portrait of a Photographer. By Arthur Lubow. New York: Harper Collins, 2016, xviii + 734 pp., $35.00 hardcover, $17.99 paperback.

Mark Gerald

Mark Gerald

211 Central Park West, Suite 1i
New York, NY 10024
Email: mgerald143@aol.com

Mark Gerald   Faculty, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, the Stephen Mitchell Relational Study Center, the Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy (New York), and the National Training Program of the National Institute for the Psychotherapies.

As a photographer and psychoanalyst, I have been intrigued by what makes a portrait memorable. How do photographer and subject collaborate in producing an iconic image? In my own work as a photographer and writer, I have explored the relational image, which can be defined as the new visual/emotional event created when two mutually vulnerable partners come to see one another in an entirely fresh way. This essay is a further exploration of the relational image, through a close examination of two biographies: Portrait of a Life: Melanie Klein and the Artists (2019), by Roger Amos, and Diane Arbus: Portrait of a Photographer (2016), by Arthur Lubow. The juxtaposition of these two remarkable women, one as the portrayed, the other as the portrayer, provides an exciting palette of contrasts and similarities in which to explore portraiture and psychoanalysis through the relational image.

The Roaring Lion

I begin, not with Klein or Arbus, but rather with Yousuf Karsh, the photographer who created the famous and iconic portrait of Winston Churchill in 1941 (Figure 1). Karsh, one of the great portraitists of the twentieth century, survived the Armenian genocide and became Canada's most renowned photographer. Karsh and Churchill were mutual partners in the 387

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

If you have been reading our series on The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott, you may notice that in this issue of JAPA Review of Books we pause before publishing the tenth of those reviews. We'll return to Winnicott in issue 70/4, with an excellent review of Volume 10 by John Foehl and Christopher Bonovitz. Here we open with three pieces—a book essay and two reviews—on other significant voices from the past.

Book Essay: Sigmund Freud-Martha Bernays Letters

Rita Teusch's essay introduces the fourth in a planned five-volume collection of letters between Freud and his soon-to-be wife Martha Bernays, exchanged during the four years of their courtship. The entire collection—thus far available only in German—is edited by Gerhard Fichtner, Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, Albrecht Hirschmüller, and Wolfgang Kloft. Readers may also wish to see Teusch's reviews of two earlier volumes in the collection (volume 2, in JAPA 62/2; volume 3, in JAPA 65/1) and Christian Maetzener's review of volume 1 (JAPA 60/5). The correspondence in volume 4 is both passionate and historically complex. Through Freud's exchange with Bernays, we glimpse the rising social and political tides of approaching war—“Traces of our complicated existence,” in Freud's words. We also see a human, loving, and at times anxious young Freud, through the early development of his eminent career, and a strong, brilliant, empathic, and patient Bernays. We echo Teusch's hope that these letters will soon be translated into English and made more widely available.

Revisiting Early Psychoanalysts

[[RunningHead]]

The Birth of Relationship Therapy: Carl Rogers Meets Otto Rank. By Robert Kramer. Giessen: Psychosozial-Verlag, 2019, 132 pp., $35.63 hardcover.

Rosemary Balsam

Rosemary Balsam

255 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06510
Email: rosemary.balsam@yale.edu

Rosemary Balsam   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale Medical School; Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; former Co-editor, JAPA Review of Books.

In most sectors of “mainstream” psychoanalysis worldwide, interest in the work of Otto Rank has almost faded from view. There have been notable journal tributes—including a highly informative issue of American Imago edited by Peter Rudnytsky in 1984, honoring Rank as a founder of that journal in 1912, and a special issue in 2012 of the American Journal of Psychoanalysis, edited by Judith Dupont, titled “Recognizing Otto Rank, an Innovator.” There is a good 1985 biography, Acts of Will: The Life and Work of Otto Rank, by E. James Lieberman, and another, published in 1958 by Rank's colleague from Philadephia, Jessica Taft. Robert Kramer, whose book I am reviewing here, is one of a handful of current leading scholars of Rank, and in chapter iii, “Otto Rank, Midwife of the Subject,” he usefully lists other writers interested in Rank, with dates of their papers and summaries of their contributions.

Kramer teaches leadership and transformative action at George Washington University and American University in Washington, D.C., and is a visiting professor at Eotvos Lorand University in Budapest. He has coedited, in a volume published in 2012, the two hundred fifty letters that passed between Freud and Rank from 1906 to 1925, documenting their close collaboration, their thinking, and their clashes (Lieberman and Kramer 2012). Also noteworthy is a 1996 book that makes Rank's rather tortuous writing and ideas accessible to the anglophone reader. Kramer now gives us this slender but intense, well-written, and fascinating book that is mostly about Rank's contributions, but additionally notes his influence on Carl Rogers's humanistic client-centered therapy and Rogers's turn to transcendence, especially at the end of his life. Kramer suggests Rogers and Rank as seminal hidden contributors to the relational trends that shape much of contemporary psychoanalysis. Rank has a stronger claim I believe, as he was steeped also in the field of psychoanalysis.

Kramer alludes throughout his book to elements of Rank's history. Once preeminent as Freud's designated “heir” (following the 1913 ouster of Jung, the first “heir”), Rank is today mostly remembered as the first paid secretary of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society in its transition from 581

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky  

Winnicott's Collected Works: A Serles of Reviews (Continued)

In this issue of JAPA Review of Books, we are pleased to join guest editors Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris in presenting the eighth review in our series on The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott. In “The Place Where We Live: Inside, Outside, Playing and Using in Perpetuity,” Steven Cooper offers his reading of Volume 8. Cooper notes Winnicott's “major theoretical revision of Freud's and Klein's views on aggression.” But as the title of his essay suggests, Cooper's focus is on Winnicott's view of “playing as therapeutic action.” An important contemporary theorist who makes innovative use of Winnicott in his own writing about creativity, Cooper emphasizes how “themes of playing and using the object” mingle with “ideas about interpretation and culture” in each of several seminal papers published during 1967-1968, the short period covered by this volume.

Elaboration of the Unconscious

In an in-depth, thoughtful, and elegant book essay spotlighting Avner Bergstein's Bion and Meltzer s Expeditions into Unmapped Mental Life (2018), Howard Levine takes the reader through a complex expansion and elaboration of the unconscious to include “the unrepresented and unrepresentable and the implications of this expansion for analytic theory and technique.” Levine's discussion of the book addresses what Bergstein calls “an unmapped” area of the mind, that is, “an inaccessible state of mind, something unconscious ‘that has never been anything else, never been conscious.’” He goes on to explain that “this area is different from the dynamic unconscious and behaves according to a set of rules and parameters ditfer-ent from those we use to conceptualize the normal-neurotic sector of the psyche.” Levine considers Bergstein's elaboration of the unconscious “one of the most accessible and immediately useful iterations” of contemporary 169

[[RunningHead]]

What Does Neuroscience Offer Psychoanalysis? Commentary on Solms's “Revision of Drive Theory”

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

990 Highland Drive, Suite 104A
Solana Beach, CA 92075
Email: sugarmanalan@gmail.com

Alan Sugarman  

This ambitious paper (Solms 2021) by one of the leading psychoanalytic researchers and most prominent modern-day neuropsy-choanalysts offers a heuristic opportunity to consider the relationship between neuroscience and psychoanalysis. Although focusing on the concept of drives, the paper is also a manifesto designed to demonstrate the broader psychoanalytic relevance of neuroscience. Given the vast amount of research data he brings to the task, as well as his prodigious knowledge of Freud, Solms provides a useful template to use in evaluating what neuroscience can and cannot offer psychoanalysis.

Considering what I understand to be his two aims, I will address both his ideas about the concept of drive and the broader contribution of neuroscience to psychoanalytic theory and practice. Toward those ends, it is useful to review what psychoanalysis is. After all, one finds fierce polemics, both on the APsaA Listserv and in the literature, about whether it is simply a clinical technique, many techniques, a theory, many theories, or a body of knowledge with many applications (see, e.g., Eagle 2021; Jaffe 2021; Sugarman 2021, in press). Nonetheless, many of us of a certain generation were drawn to psychoanalysis because it seemed to offer the most elegant model of human mental functioning, one that could help us understand our patients and guide us in our efforts to treat them. In short, psychoanalysis, first and foremost, consists of a model of the mind. To be

[[RunningHead]]

Decir lo imposible: La función de la silueta en la elaboración simbólica de la catástrofe (Saying the Impossible: The Function of the Silhouette in the Symbolic Elaboration of Catastrophe). By Carlos Moguillansky. Buenos Aires: Editorial Teseo, 2010, 220 pp., $22.00 paperback.

Israel Katz

Israel Katz

2491 Washington Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
Email: sanfranalista@gmail.com

Israel Katz   Faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

This sophisticated, courageous book attempts to write the impossible, to give word and representation to catastrophe in its social, political, and personal dimensions through the author's curiosity, engagement, and clinical experience. It calls to mind an ongoing dialogue with the work of psychoanalytic authors on trauma, the impossible to represent, issues of representability and figurability, and the functions of analysis, as not only discovering what has already been represented but also trying to give figure and image, word and representation, to trauma and the void (see Lacan, André Green, César and Sara Botella, among others). Carlos Moguillansky focuses on the possible passage from catastrophe to catastrophic change, using figures like outlines and silhouettes, in all their intricate and multiple meanings and functions. While these figures represent absence, they also open a potential transitional space for elaboration through playing, interpreting, and appropriating, making catastrophic experiences one's own through art, literature, creative endeavors, and the analytic situation. In an introduction and twenty-two chapters, the author interweaves references to art, literature, philosophy, and psychoanalysis, presenting back-and-forth movements and iterations, creating a patchwork of concepts open to questioning and elaboration. Exhibiting a sensibility almost baroque in its richness and exuberance, his writing presents and represents catastrophe and its vicissitudes in a spiral movement of playfulness and utter seriousness.

Moguillansky, a prolific writer, is a training analyst at the Buenos Aires Psychoanalytical Association (APdeBA, a component society of the IPA). He is also a professor at APdeBA-IUSAM, an institution offering psychoanalytic training within the university setting of the Instituto Universitario de Salud Mental, founded in 2005 under his presidency of APdeBA. He begins this book, Saying the Impossible, by relating catastrophe to an event that cannot be processed or understood, its memory expressed only as a chaotic emotional crisis. responseSet.title: What Happens When an Intersex Baby Happens
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Happens When an Intersex Baby Happens

Brian Kloppenberg

Brian Kloppenberg

1133 Broadway, Suite 1127
New York, NY 10010
Email: bakloppenberg@aol.com

Brian Kloppenberg  

intersexuality, nonbinary, anatomy, neutrality

Psychoanalytic efforts to think sexual difference beyond the restrictions of binary notions about anatomy and gender continue to prove challenging in both clinical practice and theory building. Claims that such binary notions are no longer applicable fail to take seriously the ongoing power of binary processes as they manifest both consciously and unconsciously. Without a capacity to think sexual difference as more than two, a capacity that resists the lure of spectrum modeling, efforts to conceptualize intersexuality and other nonbinary forms of anatomy and gender falter. An exploration into the challenges faced by two parents of an intersex baby, especially regarding the dilemma of pursuing genital surgery or not, shows some of the difficulties inherent in thinking sexual difference when the profound inadequacy of binary notions cannot be ignored.

[[RunningHead]]

From Battleground to Playground: The Video Game Avatar as Transitional Phenomenon for a Transgender Patient

Sien Rivera

Sien Rivera

15 Medical Park Road, Suite 141
Columbia, SC 29203
Email: sien.rivera@prismahealth.org

Sien Rivera  

transitional object, transitional phenomena, transitional space, object relations, gender, gender dysphoria, transgender, gender-nonconforming, virtual space, video games

The language and theories of Winnicott offer a lens for examining the importance of play in the development of the queer, transgender, and/or gender-nonconforming patient and the safety that virtual spaces—specifically, video games—provide for this play. While much of the literature posits the primary therapeutic value of virtual space as the site of psychic battle, interaction with a video game avatar can also be understood as an act of queerness-as-play, a potential cornerstone of healthy identity formation that may otherwise be precluded. An example of this paradigm is given in the case report of a twenty-year-year-old transwoman who used her video game avatar as a tool in her therapeutic progress during her medical transition. For effective psychotherapy to take place, both patient and clinician must have a capacity to play. With this in mind, the clinician might best visualize the virtual space not as a battleground, but as a playground.

responseSet.authorMast: Leon Hoffman,  Daniel Burston and  Henry J. Friedman
responseSet.authorCitation: Hoffman, L., Burston, D. & Friedman, H. J.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Moss's “On Having Whiteness”

Leon Hoffman , Daniel Burston and Henry Friedman

Henry Friedman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
Email: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

261 Maple Aveue
Pittsburgh, PA 15218
Email: burston@duq.edu

6 Garden Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138
Email: friedmanhj@aol.com

Henry Friedman  

July 6, 2021. I am perturbed by the use of the phrase “Parasitic Whiteness,” used throughout Donald Moss's “On Having Whiteness” (JAPA 69/2). Moss asserts that “the condition is foundational, generating characteristic ways of being in one's body, in one's mind, and in one's world. Parasitic Whiteness renders its hosts’ appetites voracious, insatiable, and perverse” (p. 356).

Dorothy Holmes (2021a) begins her discussion of Moss's paper by noting that she was “intrigued and made anxious” by it (p. 373) and ends by calling Moss “brave, bold, and clearheaded about Whiteness, knowing that it can so easily befuddle and upset us” (p. 380).

Was the anxiety Holmes experienced similar to mine when reading the word “parasitic”? She uses the word only once, instead interrogating the concept of Whiteness without the adjective. In her other contribution to JAPA 69/2, Holmes (2021b) discusses the embeddedness of “white supremacy” in many institutions. Moss does not use the term “white supremacy” in his discussion. How different are these two terms: “white supremacy” and “Parasitic Whiteness?” My objection to the term “Parasitic Whiteness” is that the term is reminiscent of the slur “Jewish parasite” (Wikipedia 1). In contrast, “white supremacy” is not a slur. It is a term derived from seventeenth-century scientific racism (Wikipedia 2). The latter term denigrates nonwhites and is a self-designated term of approbation which from the “19th to the mid-20th century was largely taken for granted by political leaders and social scientists in Europe and the United States” (Jenkins 2005).

In the introduction to JAPA 69/2, Mitchell Wilson (pp. 233-234) notes that psychoanalysis has a history of using pejorative terms to describe patients, such as “borderline” or “primitive.” He notes that “as analysts we know that words of significance carry history on their backs” (p. 234). Has Donald Moss introduced a new pejorative term, a slur with a long history, into a respected psychoanalytic journal?

[[RunningHead]]

Warmed by the Fires: Selected Papers of Allan Frosch. Edited by Joseph A. Cancelmo, Batya R. Monder, and Hattie B. Myers. New York: International Psychoanalytic Books (IPBooks), 2019, 360 pp., $35.00 paperback.

Jeri Isaacson

Jeri Isaacson

209 Cooper Avenue, Suite 5a
Montclair, NJ 07043
Email: jeriisaacson@gmail.com

Jeri Isaacson   Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR); Fellow, International Psychoanalytical Association.

A younger colleague recently lamented the lack of what he called “the bread and butter” of psychoanalytic training. He was referring to the current emphasis within our training institutes on the sociopolitical aspects of psychoanalysis, in contrast to what he was looking for: an invigorating 805 dive into deepening our understanding of how psychic transformation takes place in the psychoanalytic relationship. Warmed by the Fires: Selected Papers of Allan Frosch will satisfy that longing.

This volume of collected papers is a most welcome contribution to the “bread and butter” of psychoanalysis. In it, we are offered the opportunity to follow Frosch's thinking over many years as he develops a comprehensive and elegant model of clinical theory based on his understanding of how the process of symbolization takes place in the psyche and the myriad ways that this process manifests in psychoanalysis. Throughout, Frosch offers abundant clinical material with unusual intimacy. The result is a body of work that demonstrates the depths to which classical Freudian theory can still be plumbed, extending its reach to create a theory of clinical practice that is an exemplar of contemporary Freudian thinking.

Allan Frosch was a training analyst at the Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR), a beloved supervisor and instructor and a cherished colleague. He died in October 2016, at the age of seventy-eight, leaving the IPTAR community bereft. I never had the opportunity to know him. A newcomer to IPTAR, I could only bear witness to the resounding grief that reverberated through the institute upon his death. In retrospect, I see now that his loss was also accompanied by a sense of foreboding, coinciding as it did with our collective knowledge that we were on the cusp of more losses to come. The sense of loss and sorrow that has since taken over the world, and the multitude of sociopolitical changes that have taken place, have roiled all of us. This has been reflected in our psychoanalytic institutes, as new rounds of struggle to relinquish older forms take place. All of this can sometimes feel as if it is shaking the very foundations of psychoanalysis. In this context, Frosch's work is like a tonic. He offers a refreshing look at the actual work that takes place in the clinical encounter. His perspective is built on layers of carefully explicated theory that culminate in a comprehensive way of thinking, one that unites theory and practice, suggesting approaches to the clinical encounter that are, above all, practical. This volume can be used to reorient and reawaken one's clinical focus while in the midst of difficult psychoanalytic work.

[[RunningHead]]

Revision of Drive Theory: Commentary on Solms's “Revison of Drive Theory”

Paul Schwartz

Paul Schwartz

7237 Cincinnati-Dayton Road, Suite 204A
West Chester, OH 45069
Email: pjschwartz1000@gmail.com

Paul Schwartz  

Mark Solms (2021b) has recently called for numerous radical revisions to Freud's venerated drive theory. The eminent and prolific Solms needs no introduction. Among his vast array of impressive achievements are the founding in 1999 of his flagship journal Neuropsychoanalysis, his book The Brain and the Inner World (Solms and Turnbull 2002), his provocative publication “The Conscious Id” (Solms 2013), and his eagerly awaited New Project for a Scientific Psychology (see Solms 2020). However, most of Solms's proposed revisions to Freud's drive theory are based on faulty scientific reasoning and do not helpfully advance either Freud's metapsychological model of the mind or psychoanalytic clinical technique. In particular, Solms's claims that do not hold up to scientific scrutiny include (1) that Freudian unconscious phylogenetic memories don't exist; (2) that unconscious affects don't exist; (3) that human sexual behavior is driven predominantly by conscious feelings; (4) that Friston's free-energy is equivalant to Freud's drive energy; (5) that drives cannot be confluent; and (6) that consciousness resides in the id and that the ego is fundamentally unconscious. For logical coherence, I will follow in my commentary the same organizational format found in Solms 2021b.

Solms starts by quoting Freud's definition of drive “as a measure of the demand made upon the mind for work in consequence of its connection with the body (Freud 1915a)” (p. 1034). In keeping with the laws of physics, Solms then correctly states that Freud implies that this demand for work “must be driven by an energy of some kind… . Thus,

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Georges Perec's Zeit-Raum: Creating a Space of Remembrance

Anat Mahalel

Anat Mahalel

9 Haviva Reich Street
Haifa 3220705
ISRAEL
Email: anatmahalel@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2690-9146

Anat Mahalel  

Georges Perec, Walter Benjamin, dreams, Freudian theory, memory, philosophy, transitional space, trauma, unconscious

Georges Perec's memoir of his analysis, “The Scene of a Stratagem” (1977), is part of a literary oeuvre characterized by innovative forms addressing the paradoxical task of telling a story that cannot be told. His life history was constructed from memory traces, veiled behind the untimely death of his parents in World War II. The memoir tells the story of his analysis in adulthood with Jean-Bertrand Pontalis, at a time when Perec was struggling with depression and writer's block. Beneath doubts and the tedious analytic routine, Perec presents analysis as a space in which memory traces can be given new life. The historical past takes place in a space Walter Benjamin calls time-space (Zeit-raum), or timedream (Zeit-traum). This space is created in a flash of co-incidence between dreaming and waking. History, then, is created in a space where archived memory traces are transformed into present experience. Perec creates a kind of Benjaminian dream-space of the past to deal with the fragmentation of memory that follows traumatic loss. The significance of this distinct space is discussed in relation to the challenge of representing traumatic experiences while remaining faithful to the dreamlike and fragmented nature of the space.

responseSet.title: Play Changes Us: Playing the Object, Becoming the Analyst
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Play Changes Us: Playing the Object, Becoming the Analyst

Ken Corbett

Ken Corbett

30 East 21st Street
New York, NY 10010
Email: kencorbett@kencorbett.com

Ken Corbett  

The manner in which play changes us is examined, with particular attention to the role of paradox. Sustaining paradox and illusory experiencing are held as key to the framework of psychoanalysis for patient and analyst alike. Particular attention is paid to the ways in which the analyst becomes a potential object of change through playing. The transitive hospitality of playing is examined in the treatment of a four-year-old boy, and in his resurrection of a near-dead mother and her neglected child. This treatment is examined in order to better understand the preoccupation of playing within illusion, as opposed to quickly naming play content. Playing is itself transformative, prior to any reflection or interpretation that may follow.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Practicing Online During Covid-19: Psychodynamic and Psychoanalytic Therapists’ Experiences

Katie Aafjes-van Doorn , Vera Békés , Tracy Prout and Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

Rousso Building
1165 Morris Park Avenue
Bronx, NY 10461
katie.aafjes@yu.edu
https://orcid.org/GGGG-GGG3-2584-5897

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3043-5155

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3650-5890

Leon Hoffman  

Covid-19, teletherapy, survey, therapy process, challenge

During the Covid-19 pandemic, psychotherapists quickly transitioned to provide online therapy, while facing many challenges. This study aimed to explore psychodynamic and psychoanalytically oriented therapists’ (N = 1450) experiences with online therapy during the first weeks of the pandemic and two months later. Results showed that therapists had little pre-pandemic experience with providing online therapy and even less training in it, and that younger therapists reported more challenges in the transition to online therapy. During the first weeks of the pandemic, most therapists thought that online therapy was less effective than in-person therapy, and they reported a wide range of relational and technical challenges, feeling more tired, less confident and competent, and less connected and authentic in online sessions, compared to previous in-person sessions. At follow-up, therapists viewed online therapy as more comparable to in-person therapy; the majority felt connected and authentic as they had during the initial weeks of the pandemic, or more so, but were still as tired as before. The most challenging aspect of online therapy was distraction in sessions, which increased over time. This study demonstrates the professional adaptability of therapists and highlights the need for more training and professional support for clinicians providing remote psychotherapy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anti-Racist Racism: Trauma, Traumatism, Traumatophilia

Avgi Saketopoulou

Avgi Saketopoulou

80 University Place, #5C
New York, NY 10003
Email: avgisaketopoulou@gmail.com

Avgi Saketopoulou  

race, racism, traumatophilia, traumatophobia, Jean Laplanche, overwhelm, consent, queer of color critique, black feminist studies, race play

A particular epistemology of trauma now wields an outsized hold over psychoanalysis. Trauma, we are trained to think, has destructive effects whose ghostly lingerings can, nevertheless, be durably turned into ancestors. But what if we got this wrong? What if trauma is not a piece of shrapnel to be removed but a cause of becoming? And what if the tenet that trauma may be worked through is but a manifestation of a disciplinary spell? Introduction of the concept of traumatophobia invites readers to rethink the relationship of psychoanalysis to healing or repair, and to become attentive, instead, to what subjects do with trauma (traumato-philia). Traumatophilia involves a revivification of trauma, and that is easily confused with compulsive repetition or destructiveness. Traumatophobia, however, also courts psychic energies that can be psychically transformative. For transformation to be possible, however, we need to be working with a notion of psychic life that can be transformed. Careful consideration of a controversial sexual fetish (race play) shows how belated efforts of psychoanalysis to attend to racial trauma have generated new forms of racism: treating the Black unconscious not as a transformative and dynamic force but as housing only repressed representational contents. Considering race play instead through traumato-philia allows a race player's actions to be mined not for unconscious motives or for what past traumata, symbolized or unformulated, those motives carry, but to track their effects. Traumatophilic repetitions, it is argued, deliver traumatisms that may (or may not) be transformational. Questions of ethics are thus central to this paper's thinking, which is grounded in queer of color critique, Black feminisms, and Laplanchean metapsychology. Because traumatophobia preserves the world order (and thus white logics), readers are urged to consider the metapsychol-ogy of traumatophilia.

[[RunningHead]]

Maternal Erotic Transferences and the Work of the Abject

Andrea Celenza

Andrea Celenza

11 Field Road
Lexington, MA 02421
Email: acelenza@andreacelenza.com

Andrea Celenza  

transference, countertransference, death instinct, aggression, holding environment, identification, individuation, internalization, introjection, unconscious, transitional space

Kristeva's profound and comprehensive understanding of maternal eroticism allows us to examine the ways in which a negative maternal transference, structured through the work of the abject, can be viewed as a maternal erotic transference in its devitalizing form. Through the use of a clinical case, the revitalizing experiences of maternal eroticism within analytic process facilitate the emergence of a maternal erotic transference in its vitalizing form. The lack of erotic dimensions in maternal transferences, it is argued, may be viewed as present absences, and the development of a positive maternal erotic transference as an achievement. André Green's visual-spatial metaphor of foreclosed space is extended as an imploding force wherein abject signifiers do their devitalizing work. In contrast, the usable space of a healthy psychoanalytic process is viewed as the envelope around which abject signifiers emerge, becoming available for psychoanalytic work. A transformation related to the author's experiences with her dying mother is shown to have expanded her containing capacities, including new areas of patience and receptivity. These dynamics are placed within the context of a multiverse conception of maternal eroticism, including the analyst's disciplined receptivity, viewed as an eternal signifier of the maternal.

[[RunningHead]]

When the Good Object is also a Thief: A Memoir of Adoption

Jennifer Guittard

Jennifer Guittard

1939 Divisadero. Suite 1B
San Francisco, CA 94115
Email: drjguittard@aol.com

Jennifer Guittard  

adoption triad, adoption-specific maternal representation, parenting and adoption, dysregulation and adoption, shame in adoption, behavioral issues in adopted children, maternal representations, psychodynamics in adoption, Kleinian and contemporary Kleinian theory and adoption, adoption

This contemporary Kleinian memoir explores the possible existence of an intrapsychic, adoption-specific preoedipal triad including child, birth mother, and adoptive mother that can shape the emerging mind. As an intrapsychic construct, the adoption triad comes to exist in the infantile mind, requiring that adoptees contend with four additional part-object maternal representations: a villain (bad birth mother), a victim (good birth mother), a rescuer (good adoptive other), and a thief (bad adoptive mother). The psychic complexities of this possible adoption triad are explored, with an eye to how it might illuminate the psychosocial challenges experienced by some adoptees, including dysregulated behavior, rage, dissociation, and shame. To this end, Bion's ideas regarding presymbolic, nondefensive communication and Winnicott's understanding of use of the object are invoked. Expanding the preoedipal paradigm of adoption to include the possibility of an intrapsychic, adoption-specific maternal triad can enhance our understanding of the psychology of adoption, as well as highlight the need to consider the ways in which internal objects can exist simultaneously in both dyadic and triadic paradigms.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“The Moon of the Magic Casement”: Transitional Phenomena, the Moon, and D. W. Winnicott's Death

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 9, 1969-1971. Edited by Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2016.

Ken Corbett

Ken Corbett

30 East 21st Street
New York, NY 10010
Email: kencorbett@kencorbett.com

Ken Corbett   Faculty, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

What is the phrase for the moon? And the phrase for love? By what name are we to call death?

—Virginia Woolf, The Waves

And so with Volume 9 (1969-1971) of D. W. Winnicott's Collected Works, we come to the end: “1971 - Dies of a coronary in January” (CW, Chronology, p. 402).

373

This fatal clotting was the last knot in a string of heart attacks beginning in 1948, including a serious coronary Winnicott suffered after presenting “The Use of the Object” to the New York Psychoanalytic Society on November 12, 1968. He was hospitalized in New York for five weeks, returning to London just before the dawn of 1969.

Volume 9 breathes under the two-year shadow of Winnicott's hovering death, and cannot be read outside of that certainty. At the same time, the volume pulses with a life of speculation, revision, contemplation, and completion. Of particular note are the papers that Winnicott worked to collect in Playing and Reality (1971), including his final revision of “Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena,” originally presented at a scientific meeting of the British Psychoanalytical Society in 1951 (CW 3:6:6), then revised and published in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis in 1953 (CW 4:2:21), and republished in Winnicott's 1958 book, Through Pediatrics to Psychoanalysis (CW 5:4:24). Winnicott's twenty-year reworking of his ideas about transitional phenomena points to the centrality of these observations in his theorizing about human “growth” (his preferred term), along with his propositions about therapeutic action.

Transitional phenomena, and the consideration thereof, have also become a cornerstone of modern psychoanalytic thought. “Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena” has been the most popular psychoanalytic journal article for many years running, requested 3,755 times in 2020 alone (PEP Web). Playing and Reality sits on most every analyst's bookshelf; my copy, read and reread, is held together by masking tape and string. Approximately 70,000 English language copies of Playing and Reality have been sold. It has been translated into Bulgarian, Chinese, Czech, Danish, French, German, Greek, Hebrew, Hungarian, Italian, Japanese, Korean, Lithuanian, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Spanish, Swedish, Taiwanese, and Turkish (Camilla Ferrier, the Marsh Agency, personal communication, August, 2020).

[[RunningHead]]

The Power of Stories

Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI 148009
Email: nkulish@aol.com

Nancy Kulish  

female psychology, identity, neuroscience, oedipus complex, Native American

Stories hold an important role in the theory and practice of psychoanalysis. Their power derives in part from the fact that humans are biologically programmed for storytelling. Stories foster connection with others, socialization, and identity formation. They are integral to all psychoanalytic theories of therapeutic action, and their telling is therapeutic in itself. Psychoanalytic theoretical paradigms can be considered stories, as well. A positive outcome of analytic treatment has the individual emerging with an open-ended story that encompasses past, present, and future. Finally, an autobiographical example demonstrates how shared stories consolidate group identities, uphold and perpetuate a group's power over others, and perpetuate racial and ethnic stereotypes.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Neutrality, Race, and Racism

Stephen Portuges

Stephen Portuges

2014 Sawtelle Boulevard
Los Angeles, CA 90025-6230
Email: sportuges@comcast.net

Stephen Portuges  

neutrality, race, racism, white supremacy, ideology, color blind racism

The white man is sealed in his whiteness, the black man in his blackness… . How do we extricate ourselves?

—Franz Fanon, Black Skin, White Masks

So what does it mean to consider the concept of psychoanalytic neutrality in the clinical setting “as a white lie”? Why a lie at all, even if it is only a white one that allegedly does no harm? And what does a white lie have to do with race and racism? I want to explore these questions and some of the important issues they raise. I'll begin with some ideas about the political effects of applying the neutrality principle to the development of clinical psychoanalysis. Then I want to consider how centuries of racial inequality in the U.S. have been legitimated and justified during different phases of a sociopolitical system of white supremacy that has used white lies, among many other tricks, to sustain white domination over people of color (see especially Crenshaw 1995, 2011, 2019; Lipsitz 2016, 2019; Mills 1997). Finally, I want to consider the idea that neutrality may function for white analysts as an ideological1 1Elsewhere I have discussed ideology as “a systematic scheme of ideas that reflect important political, moral values and beliefs”; developed in and communicated through “social institutions like the church, the state, the family, public and professional media, and educational [organizations],” ideologies are normative “maps of the psychosocial world that … mandate the terms of … theoretical and clinical discourse, stipulating what can be legitimately thought and said and demarcating the criteria of authorized therapeutic exchange” (Portuges 2009, p. 61). As important as it is in explicating and shaping social reality, ideology is seldom dis-embedded in our theories of mind and unwrapped in our clinical work, especially in formal psychoanalytic education.

New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles.

Paper presented to plenary panel “Neutrality as a ‘White Lie,’” American Psychoanalytic Association, February 14, 2021.

Neutrality as a “White Lie”: Discussion of Gabbard, Holmes, and Portuges

Anton Hart

Anton Hart

235 West End Avenue, Suite 2E
New York, NY 10023
Email: antonhartphd@gmail.com

Anton Hart  

neutrality, whiteness, radical openness, equipoise, abstinence

How do I usefully discuss these three thoughtful and illuminating contributions on the central, if increasingly questionable, concept of neutrality in psychoanalysis? I proceed from an attitude of respect and admiration toward each of my distinguished colleagues, Glen Gabbard, Dorothy Holmes, and Stephen Portuges, regarding their contributions to psychoanalytic thought overall, and regarding what they have offered for our consideration of this matter of neutrality at the present moment in the intertwined evolution of psychoanalysis and society.

It is incumbent on citizens and psychoanalytic thinkers concerned about the humanity-subverting forces of repression and oppression, whenever and wherever present, intrapsychically or interpersonally,1 to scrutinize aspects of psychoanalytic thought that may harbor discriminatory dynamics, including those that involve the valuing of one category of personhood over others. Psychoanalysts, trained to notice the ways their patients may be unconsciously subverting themselves (problems of 1The word interpersonally is used here to refer to the entire range of forms of human relatedness, from the dyadic to those constituted by groups, organizations, and social systems small and large. I intend to include in the category of the interpersonal those categories of systemic human relatedness that are often referred to as “structural,” as in structural racism or sexism, for example.

Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Institute for Psychiatry, Psychoanalysis, and Psychology; faculty, Psychology Department, Mt. Sinai Morningside/West; guest faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Paper presented to plenary panel “Neutrality as a ‘White Lie,’” American Psychoanalytic Association, February 14, 2021.

Invited paper received February 28, 2022; accepted March 7, 2022.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Only Fag Around: Twinship Needs in Gay Childhood

Sam Guzzardi

Sam Guzzardi

32 Union Square East, Suite 1106
New York, NY 10003
Email: sgpsychotherapy@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9191-1007

Sam Guzzardi  

sexuality, selfobject transference, self psychology, development, repetition, transference, working through, intersubjectivity

A number of contemporary psychoanalytic writers have characterized gay childhood as a profoundly isolating experience. Within a developmentally informed self psychological framework, the loneliness of gay childhood is theorized here as a deficit in requisite twinship experience in early life. A detailed clinical example illustrates how these thwarted twinship needs may reemerge in the transference to the analyst, and how patients may escalate their acting out when the analyst misattunes to, or altogether misses, manifestations of twinship longings in the transference. A bridge between Freud's theory of the repetition compulsion and Kohut's theory of selfobject transferences suggests how specific moments of thwarted twinship needs may be repeated within the analytic relationship in an attempt to master the earlier experience. The experience of the analyst being pulled into the patient's attempts at mastery is detailed. A broader theoretical trend is hypothesized whereby psychoanalytic theories may have a pull toward twinship between each other.

[[RunningHead]]

Neutrality is not Neutral

Dorothy Holmes

Dorothy Holmes

10 Pinckney Colony Road
Suite 315, Bldg. 300
Bluffton, SC 29909
Email: crescent@gwu.edu

Dorothy Holmes  

neutrality, race, culture

Psychoanalytic discourse on the concept of neutrality is long-standing and controversial. In current times, neutrality is an evolved concept that garners some respect, but is also held suspect to the extent that it is rooted in a positivist framework for science requiring psychoanalysis to exclude extraneous factors such as the analyst's subjectivities and external reality. To become optimally neutral within this framework, analysts are required to cure themselves of extraneous factors in their own analyses. Freud's investment in the positivist approach, including the analyst's being neutral, was intended to curb the analyst's overambitious emotional stake in the patient's progress (Freud 1915). Apropos the analyst's subjectivities, the field of psychoanalysis has made a vast shift toward their recognition and engagement in psychoanalytic thinking and practice. On the issue of the place of external reality as a necessary focus of the analyst's neutrality, a signature contribution was made by Anna Freud, who viewed neutrality as an attitude of equipoised interest in all agencies of the patient's mind, as well as in the patient's external reality. So in modern psychoanalytic thinking, neutrality is not neutral as would have been required by a positivist view of science. The positivistic notions requiring a sterilely neutral analyst are considered antiquated, and in some instances harmful, with the potential to produce undue and unanalyzable regression in patients (Levy and Inderbitzen 1992).

I found it necessary to prepare this essay because of one way in which I have experienced a regressive shift toward the antiquated demand that the analyst be neutral. Specifically, I have frequently been exhorted to be more neutral in my work with patients regarding their interest and focus,

Private practice.

Paper presented to plenary panel “Neutrality as a ‘White Lie,’” American Psychoanalytic Association, February 14, 2021.

Invited paper submitted March 21, 2022; accepted March 22, 2022.

[[RunningHead]]

The Decline and Fall of Neutrality in Psychoanalytic Discourse

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

4306 Yoakum Boulevard, Suite 535
Houston, TX 77006
Email: glen.gabbard@gmail.com

Glen Gabbard  

neutrality, multiplicity, countertransference, openness, transference

It has been over eighty-five years since Anna Freud published her widelyregarded definition of neutrality (1936), which at the time was a cornerstone of psychoanalytic technique. She conceived of it as a position in which the analyst remained equidistant from the psychic agencies id (desires and wishes), ego (the demands of reality), and superego (the demands of society's morality) and from external reality factors. When I was a candidate learning to speak the jargon of fully trained analysts in the past century, I read this definition and thought to myself, “How am I going to keep track of all these competing forces?” As I went on in my training, I began to recognize that the constructs of id, ego, and superego are not that easily identified. They are simply metaphors for aspects of our internal world that may or may not be reliably present.

The concept of superego, for example, can be difficult to pin down. Jacob Arlow (1982) once critiqued the superego, with tongue firmly in cheek, as follows: “The superego is by no means a uniform, coherent, integrated harmonious structure. It is a mass of contradictions, fraught with internal inconsistency, or, as we say in our technical language, intrasystemic conflicts. Its functioning is neither uniform nor reliable. It is in this respect that the idea that the superego represents the policeman of the

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Baylor College of Medicine.

Paper presented to plenary panel “Neutrality as a ‘White lie,’” American Psychoanalytic Association, February 14, 2021.

Invited paper received February 20, 2022; accepted February 27, 2022.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of Suicide, Part I: An Integration of Contemporary Theory and Research

Mark Schechter , Mark Goldblatt , Elsa Ronningstam and Benjamin Herbstman

Benjamin Herbstman

81 Highland Avenue
Salem, MA 01970-2768
Email: mschechter@partners.org

Benjamin Herbstman  

suicide, psychoanalytic, entrapment, emotional pain, escape, implicit processes

Psychodynamic psychotherapy has an important role in suicide prevention. The psychoanalytic study of suicide has taught us a great deal about the human experience and the process of suicidality. There is also much to be learned from other fields of study and from empirical research that can be integrated into psychoanalytic therapies. Central to the psychoanalytic approach to suicide has been understanding the patient's internal subjective experience of unbearable emotional or psychic pain and the urgent need for relief. Emotional pain can include intense affects such as shame, humiliation, self-hate, and rage. Factors that can increase vulnerability to suicidal states include problems with early attunement, dissociation and deficits in bodily love and protection, conscious and unconscious fantasy, and certain character traits and dynamics. Empirical research has confirmed many basic psychoanalytic concepts about suicide, including escape from unbearable pain as the primary driver of suicidal behavior, the role of dissociation in increasing risk of bodily attack, and the importance of unconscious processes. Further research into implicit processes and their role in the suicidal process holds potential to improve suicide risk assessment and to enhance psychotherapy by bringing otherwise inaccessible material into the treatment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of Suicide, Part II: An Integration of Theory, Research, and Clinical Practice

Mark Schechter , Mark Goldblatt , Elsa Ronningstam and Benjamin Herbstman

Benjamin Herbstman

81 Highland Avenue
Salem, MA 01970-2768
Email: mschechter@partners.org

Benjamin Herbstman  

suicide, psychoanalytic, entrapment, emotional pain, escape, implicit processes

In Part I contemporary psychoanalytic concepts about suicide were synthesized with other theories and empirical research findings. Here the focus is on applying those principles and describing an integrative psychodynamic approach to treatment, one emphasizing the therapeutic alliance, unconscious and implicit processes, exploration of fantasy, and use of the therapeutic relationship as an implicitly interpretive vehicle for change. It is “integrative” because it draws on ideas and techniques described in dialectical behavioral therapy (DBT) and cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), as well on developmental and social psychology research. Psychotherapy with suicidal patients is inherently challenging, requiring the therapist to bear intense emotional pain while attending to potentially derailing countertransference pressures. The therapist plays an active role in helping the patient navigate affect storms and counter harsh self-attack, and instilling hope that treatment can lead to meaningful change. The integrative psychodynamic approach offers a pathway to a lessening of harsh self-judgment, greater connection with the body, improvement in continuity of experience, positive changes in narrative identity, emergence of the patient's genuine capacities, and more satisfying interpersonal relationships. These changes promote affect tolerance, improve life satisfaction, and decrease the likelihood of suicidal behavior.

Howard Levine   Faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East, and NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, Contemporary Freudian Track.

Bion's journey in thinking about an ineffable psychic reality, starting with his depiction of a psychotic part of the personality up until his conceptualization of an ultimate reality which he denotes as O … is a path leading from the notion of a part of the personality that lacks the capacity to be in contact with emotional reality and truth, to an apprehension of an ineffable, unthinkable psychic reality which is beyond the reach of human capacity.

—Avner Bergstein (2019, p. xi)

It is hard to imagine a concept more central to psychoanalysis than that of the unconscious. Indeed, Freud believed that affirming the existence of unconscious mental processes was an obligatory cornerstone of psychoanalytic theory.1 While few, if any, in a psychoanalytic audience would doubt or question that assumption, after more than a century of analytic practice, we continue to find ourselves debating the very nature, organization, and structure of the unconscious, as we struggle to fully explore, operationalize, and describe all that we mean by this foundational term. The expansion and elaboration of our understanding of the unconscious to include the unrepresented and unrepresentable, and the implications of 1“The assumption that there are unconscious mental processes, the recognition of the theory of resistance and repression, the appreciation of the importance of sexuality and of the Oedipus Complex—these constitute the principal subject matter of psycho-analysis and the foundations of its theory” (Freud 1923, p. 247).187

[[RunningHead]]

Stance, Set, Transference: The Differentiation of Two Modes of Clinical Technique

Andrea Celenza

Andrea Celenza

11 Field Road
Lexington, MA 02421
Email: acelenza@andreacelenza.com

Andrea Celenza  

creativity, dreams, evenly hovering attention, interpretation, repetition, reverie, transference, unconscious

Comparative approaches to psychoanalytic theory are a major source of exposure to different theoretical orientations and clinical technique. However, it is not always clear what the analyst actually does when translating theory into practice. As an attempt at clarification, descriptions are provided of two listening stances or attentional sets that are associated with different modes of analytic listening. These modes reside at a relatively low level of abstraction and are experience-near. The two listening stances are (1) a directed attentional set aimed at the identification of conscious or unconscious repetitious patterns, and (2) a diffuse attentional set receptive to emergent phenomena for the purpose of elaborating unconscious fantasy. These listening modes are combined in everyday practice and usually occur in an oscillating fashion; thus, separation of these listening modes in this discussion is for heuristic purposes. At the same time, these modes are prioritized differently among various theoretical models depending on the analyst's intention and goals. They also can be correlated with different types of transferences, evoking different types of clinical material that correspond to the analyst's goals and/or to phases in treatment. Because these attentional modes are governed by different neurophysiological substrates, it is helpful for analysts to have these modes clearly differentiated in their minds, even though engaging these modes tends to be spontaneous and rapidly oscillating. Two clinical vignettes are provided that illustrate the use of these two modes of listening where one or the other is prioritized.

[[RunningHead]]

Neutrality as a “White Lie”: Introduction

Stephen Portuges

Stephen Portuges

2014 Sawtelle Boulevard
Los Angeles, CA 90025-6230
Email: sportuges@comcast.net

Stephen Portuges  

The four invited papers we are publishing in this issue of JAPA under the heading “Neutrality as a ‘White Lie’” are minor modifications of papers presented at an APsaA plenary panel with the same title on February 14, 2021. We thought it might be useful for readers to know how the papers’ authors arrived at this unusual title.

During our final organizational meeting several months before the panel convened and shortly after having been selected by APsaA's Program Committee to discuss neutrality, race, and racism, Glen Gabbard, Anton Hart, Dorothy Holmes, and I were trying to frame the many technical and ethical issues involved in addressing race and racism in the clinical setting. We had just taken up the conjecture that our profession had reified its aspirations to do no harm by idealizing neutrality because it promised to keep clinical psychoanalysts from unduly influencing patients. Although we had different conceptions of the value of the neutrality principle for psychoanalytic treatment, as our conversation delved into the ethical implications of exploring racial identity and treating racism in ourselves and our patients, Anton Hart wondered if we had been preconsciously contemplating psychoanalytic neutrality itself as what he called “a white lie.” Not yet appreciating the multiple meanings of that phrase, we nevertheless agreed that Hart's spot-on three-word condensation of our exchange could serve as a platform for considering how the psychoanalytic principle of neutrality influences the analysis of race and racism in the clinical setting. The following articles are products of our collective and individual deliberations on this important topic. We are indebted to Anton Hart for having insightfully captured the complexities of our conversation in a single phrase.
