[[RunningHead]]

Adolescent Ruthlessness and the Transitioning of the Analyst's Mind

Oren Gozlan

Oren Gozlan

507 Davenport Road
Toronto, M4V 1B
CANADA
Email: ogozlan@gozlanpsych.com

Oren Gozlan  

identification, object relations, gender, desire, counter-transference, ruthlessness, transgender, nonbinary

Features of ruthlessness may come into play in the encounter between adolescents who come into the therapeutic space with clear and precise demands to be supported through their gender transition. Winnicott's concept of ruthlessness, extended from the infant-mother matrix to the emotional situation of the clinic, allows a consideration of the conditions under which the analyst can think about adolescent demands. These conditions involve the desire of both patient and analyst for certitude and the analyst's urgency to respond, as well as the adolescent's contradictory desires to both destroy and create gender. The work of Sally Swartz, who brought to Winnicott's conception a notion of ruthlessness in protest, helps us consider the qualities of ruthlessness in constituting gender. A snippet of work with a nonbinary patient shows how questions of gender cannot be understood apart from the intersubjective transferential field. Tying ruthlessness to the enigma of desire illuminates the emotional situation of the clinical encounter between the nonbinary or trans patient and the analyst, a situation that is also libidinal. An analytic move from the question of gender identity to the realm of an emotional situation allows the analyst to meet adolescent ruthlessness. This meeting is an ethical attempt to understand the other, but also reveals one's resistance to giving up something in order to understand.

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2403-0549

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

In this mid-year issue, we have taken the opportunity to examine the state of psychoanalysis at the present moment, both here and abroad, as we explore complex questions regarding our changing world and its effect on training, on theory, and on technique. We look at psychoanalysts present and past who have considered these questions from within their particular sociopolitical scope, and we examine the role of the written word as historical marker, as narrative record, and as vessel for human experience. We look forward to resuming our series on The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott with an excellent upcoming review of Volume 10 by John Foehl and Christopher Bonovitz.

Julia Kristeva: Perspectives on Her Life and Work

Laura Kleinerman's book essay on Julia Kristeva's Je me voyage: Mémoires, entretiens avec Samuel Dock and Alice Jardine's At the Risk of Thinking: An Intellectual Biography of Julia Kristeva is an admirable example of the inevitable merging of psychoanalysis and the sociopolitical environment. Kleinerman suggests that these books each paint Kristeva's life and work as inseparable by weaving together the personal and intellectual trajectory of one of the most prominent thinkers of our time. Kleinerman follows Kristeva's biographical and intellectual journey by crossing borders, disciplines, and even the facets of the mind that psychoanalysis reveals. By following this journey, Kleinerman aims to highlight, in particular, three of Kristeva's major contributions to the psychoanalytic field: feminine estrangement, abjection, and maternal reliance.

Rudnytsky Revives Ferenczi and Severn

In an appreciative essay, Richard Waugaman, an analyst who has written extensively on English literature, considers two recent works by Peter Rudnytsky, psychoanalyst, professor of English at the University of Florida, and former editor-in-chief of American Imago. In Formulated 757

Otto Kernberg   Personality Disorders Institute, New York Presbyterian Hospital, Westchester Division; Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Psychotherapy for Psychosis is an original and carefully elaborated contribution to psychoanalytic psychotherapy for patients suffering from chronic delusional illness. Michael Garrett makes it clear that he includes a broad spectrum of patients, with various types of chronic schizophrenic, schizoaffective, and paranoid psychoses, with a particular focus on a subgroup of these patients with chronic psychotic illness for whom he proposes the treatment he has developed. He does not sharply circumscribe this select group of patients, but throughout the abundant clinical examples in this volume the preconditions that implicitly characterize the overall treatment situation and the common features of the clinical descriptions of these patients combine in a particular clinical perspective.

These are patients with chronic psychotic illness—hallucinations, delusions, alterations in linguistic expression, and severe disturbances in behavior and social relations—yet who maintain the capacity for specific affective interactions in their intimate and familial relationships. They generally evince no chronic antisocial behaviors, have at least normal intelligence, and, in spite of long-term adequate treatment with antipsychotic medications, have not been able to restore normal reality testing. It is a large group of patients, in whose cases the appropriate psychopharmacological treatment, combined with various degrees of supportive psychotherapeutic interventions and efforts at social rehabilitation, have been unable to reduce their symptomatology sufficiently to permit them to function at a nonpsychotic level, with restoration of adequate reality testing.

1685 Beacon Street, Suite 3

Rodrigo Barahona   Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Board Member, the Boston Group for Psychoanalytic Studies.

The world was a different place when Marcelo Viñar began writing Psychoanalytic Experiences in the Sociocultural Present. Before the pandemic, the vicissitudes of the subject in the rapidly shifting contexts of the last two centuries always linked to familiar events, the emblematic social upheavals and societal catastrophes of which much had already been written. Now, mid-pandemic (we think, we hope), the world is indeed changed, but the subjectivities under the microscope of Viñar's worried gaze remain the same. Perhaps it is that some invariance of personality requires more than a catastrophic event to alter the new mix of compositions of the twenty-first century selves emerging in our consulting rooms since before the pandemic. These new and fluid compositions have been hard won through slow time and by dint of the cumulative press of change. Upon finishing this book, readers will have no doubt of Viñar's attunement to how the present moment may shape the internal lives of post-pandemic generations. In these pages, they will find an author who sees the world at a sociocultural crossroads. It is at this intersection, characterized by what he calls “civilizational vertigo,” that Viñar brings to bear his wise, often agnostic, lens.

Viñar writes at the start of his book that he wants to interrogate the subject of today's liquid modernity, a phrase coined by Zygmunt Bauman. “Liquid” refers to the inconsistency or dispersion of definitions that modifies social links, including those of life, love, work, and customs. He shows how, far from being stimulated in this new, socially mediated world, diversity is suppressed, as dialogue quickly escalates into conflict and censorship, and spaces of sameness expand at the expense of messy but personal human encounters. Viñar provides us plenty of personal information to offset the erroneous impression that we are reading the words of a disillusioned psychoanalyst who feels out of step with a changing world (though he might, now in his eighties, not disagree with this diagnosis). His grandparents had fled persecution from Europe, and he 789

Sequels

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower

15 West 75th Street, Apt. 8B

Joyce Slochower  

attachment, countertransference, defense, development, individuation, separation, termination

The termination ideal for analytic work stands at a considerable distance from clinical reality; “complete” terminations are rare indeed. This gap is perhaps best explored by considering sequels—instances in which exanalyst and ex-patient become nonsexual friends post treatment. The meanings and implications of these post-termination friendships—their complexities, problematic edges, serious difficulties, and the potential therapeutic benefit of this deviation from the termination ideal—are illustrated using the unfinished story of a good treatment and its yet-tobe-defined ending. Thus, the psychoanalytic model of termination is interrogated, and the complexities of treatment sequels, for both patient and analyst, are elucidated.

Lynne Zeavin , Mary McClure , Forrest Hamer and M. Davids

M. Davids  

As the pandemic has deepened and the pervasive mood of uncertainty in the country has taken hold, we turned to a reconsideration of the problem of mourning and its vicissitudes, to what allows mourning to take place and what can impede it, both psychically and in society. This panel was held virtually in September 2021, in the wake of the twentieth anniversary of 9/11. It was meant in part to be a revisiting of Freud's 1917 foundational text, “Mourning and Melancholia.” We aimed to situate that text within the historical and social time we now inhabit as citizens and psychoanalysts. Mourning is crucial to psychic development and yet within psychoanalytic training the study of mourning often gets short shrift. Mourning is an intricate psychic process: If too threatened by the pain of loss and sorrow or the guilt to which it gives rise, it can give way to melancholia on the one hand or manic solutions on the other. If navigated and tolerated, it can lead to reparation and psychic growth.

This panel was conceived in the wake of the January 6 insurrection at the Capitol. In the coverage that followed, a phrase we all likely know was back in circulation. Insidious and rageful expressions of whitesupremacist rhetoric were met with the claim, “This is not who we are.” But “This is not who we are” is a stance that refuses mourning. It is a

Jeffrey Berman

Jeffrey Berman

Albany, NY 12222
Email: jberman@albany.edu

Jeffrey Berman   Distinguished Teaching Professor of English, University at Albany, State University of New York; Honorary Member, American Psychoanalytic Association.

“In my mind,” Sigmund Freud confided to Wilhelm Stekel as they hiked through the forests of Berchtesgaden, “I always construct novels, using my experiences as a psychoanalyst; my wish is to become a novelist—but not yet; perhaps in the later years of my life” (Stekel 1950, p. 66). Freud never became a novelist, but in Studies on hysteria (1895)(he observes ruefully that “it still strikes me myself as strange that the case histories I write should read like short stories and that, as one might say, they lack the serious stamp of science” (p. 160).

Other psychoanalysts have become novelists, and their stories raise fascinating questions about the relationship between life and art. Can one tell, from internal textual evidence alone, that the author of a novel is a psychoanalyst? Nietzsche claimed that every philosophy—and every theory—is the confession of its originator, and thus unconscious autobiography. Is the same true of fiction: unconscious autobiography? I wondered about these questions while reading Thomas Ogden's three novels: The Parts Left Out (2014), The Hands of Gravity and Chance (2016), and This Will Do … (2021).

Thomas Ogden is one of the world's most acclaimed psychoanalysts. A graduate of the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute, where he serves on the faculty, he is Director of the Center for the Advanced Study of the Psychoses. Mark Solms

Mark Solms

Mark Solms  

I am grateful for the interest in my paper. I will respond to these two commentaries in the order I received them.

Alan Sugarman

In the introductory section of his commentary, Alan Sugarman expresses disappointment that I did not outline a general psychology in my paper. Having come to the view that it is “a manifesto designed to demonstrate the broader psychoanalytic relevance of neuroscience” (p. 1139), he says that it does not properly address such fundamental topics as development, pathogenesis, and mutative action; it ignores much of developmental and cognitive psychology; and it “goes from neurobiology and chemistry directly to mental experience” (p. 1141). These are unexpected criticisms of a paper focused on drive theory, and which makes no claim to present a general psychology. In the circumstances, I can only suggest that Sugarman reads what I have written—within this same theoretical frame-work—on those other topics that interest him (e.g., Solms 2017a,b, 2018a,b,c, 2019a,b). The same applies to what I have written on the topic of dreams, which might prompt Sugarman to reconsider his view that neurophysiology “does not provide any understanding into the meaning of dreams” (p. 1143). I think it fair to say that my work on this topic has reestablished the fact, long denied by other neuroscientists, that dreams are meaningful and motivated (see, e.g., Solms 1997, 2000a,b, 2001a,b, 2004, 2009, 2012, 2014; Solms and Hobson 2006; Solms and Turnbull 2006; Malcolm-Smith et al. 2012).

Director of Neuropsychology, University of Cape Town and Groote Schuur Hospital; Co-Chair, International Neuropsychoanalysis Society; Science Director, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Playing with the Piggle

The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott: Volume 11, Human Nature and the Piggle. Edited by Lesley Caldwell and Helen Taylor Robinson. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

Judith Yanof

Judith Yanof

25 Somerset Road
West Newton, MA 02465-2721
Email: judyyanof@gmail.com

Judith Yanof   Training and Supervising Analyst, Child Analytic Supervisor, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

Gabrielle: “I want to see Dr. Winnicott. This time I will really tell him what's the matter.”

Mother: “Lucky that you know, most people don't.”

Gabrielle: “I don't know, but I can always tell him.”

—The Piggle (p. 295)

Volume 11 of The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott contains two works: The Piggle: An Account of the Psychoanalytic Treatment of a Little Girl and Human Nature. Both were unfinished at the time of Winnicott's death in January 1971, and both were published posthumously, in 1977 and 1988, respectively. My remarks will focus on The Piggle.

Reading The Piggle again after many years, I found myself, once more, fully engaged in this riveting account of a collaboration between a master child analyst and a very young, extremely precocious child, and her concerned and appealing parents. The Piggle is a remarkable clinical contribution. One hundred twenty-five pages long, it consists primarily of Winnicott's personal notes from his treatment of the Piggle (Gabrielle), a child he saw for sixteen sessions from the age of two years, five months, to the age of five years, two months. Gabrielle's parents called her Piggle, a common term of endearment for children in England. Because the child lived at a distance from London, Winnicott saw her at intervals of several weeks or even, at times, several months.

He calls this treatment “on demand,” implying that he would see the child only as she needed to be seen. Yet there were times that Gabrielle could not see Winnicott, even when she was struggling and wanted to see him. The parents were in close contact with the analyst, however, and they functioned as a bridge, providing the necessary support for an 1191

[[RunningHead]]

If Thou Follow Thy Star: A Reading of Mitchell Wilson

The Analyst's Desire: The Ethical Foundation of Clinical Practice. By Mitchell Wilson. New York: Bloomsbury Academic, 2020, xxvi + 234 pp., $115.00 hardcover.

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1
Pavia 27100
ITALY
Email: gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese   Training and Supervising Analyst, Italian Psychoanalytic Society and American Psychoanalytic Association.

“wow, man. I've never heard you play that before,” writes

Whitney Balliett, long-time jazz critic for The New Yorker, here quoted by Mitchell Wilson (p. 113), about the drummer Big Sid Catlett.

The Voice Endures

Similarly, when reading The Analyst s Desire the reader immediately realizes that the author, as is mostly said about novelists, has a “voice.” By this expression we usually mean someone who possesses “a way of engaging rhythm, timing, and accent” in a personal, original, distinct, vivid, and direct writing style—that “captures.” What? Of course, the reader's desire, caught up in the erotic game of unveiling that is generated in the Barthesian game between plaisir (the expected) and jouissance (the unexpected). Mitchell Wilson's style is simple and direct, but does not renounce a certain pleasant sophistication. It is not for nothing that he uses words like “strangerness,” Nebenmensch, “vagaries,” “gyroscopic,” “futural,” “opprobrium,” ressentiment, poesis, “unbeknownst,” chutzpah, “win-someness,” etc.

The experience of reading this book, chapter after chapter, is that of an intimate conversation: passionate, engaging, and smooth. Even before the specific content, one feels its musical structure. There are moments of agreement and others of dissonance, but they are always harmoniously inserted in the same coherent score, that is, always functional to an alternating game of convergence and divergence between author and reader that allows for development in both. After all, it is but the dance of mutual 1205
